---

### 4.  Maximum affordable loan  

Rounded to the nearest dollar,

\[
\boxed{\$89\,035}
\]

is the largest principal the buyer can afford under the given continuous‑compounding, continuous‑payment mortgage.  

#### 89035

problem_text:
A spring is stretched 6 in by a mass that weighs $8 \mathrm{lb}$. The mass is attached to a dashpot mechanism that has a damping constant of $0.25 \mathrm{lb} \cdot \mathrm{s} / \mathrm{ft}$ and is acted on by an external force of $4 \cos 2 t \mathrm{lb}$.
If the given mass is replaced by a mass $m$, determine the value of $m$ for which the amplitude of the steady state response is maximum.
teacher_cot:
The static stretchgives the spring constant  

\[
k=\frac{\text{weight}}{\text{stretch}}=\frac{8\ \text{lb}}{6\text{ in}}=
\frac{8}{0.5}\ \frac{\text{lb}}{\text{ft}}=16\ \frac{\text{lb}}{\text{ft}} .
\]

For a forced damped oscillator  

\[
m x''+c x'+k x = F_0\cos(\omega t),
\]

the steady‑state amplitude is  

\[
A(\omega)=\frac{F_0}{\sqrt{(k-m\omega^{2})^{2}+(c\omega)^{2}}}.
\]

Here \(F_0=4\) lb, \(\omega =2\) rad/s, and \(c=0.25\) lb·s/ft.  
To maximize the amplitude we minimize the denominator:

\[
D(m)=\sqrt{(k-m\omega^{2})^{2}+(c\omega)^{2}}.
\]

Since \((c\omega)^{2}\) is constant, the minimum occurs when  

\[
k-m\omega^{2}=0\quad\Longrightarrow\quad m=\frac{k}{\omega^{2}}.
\]

Thus  

\[
m=\frac{16\ \text{lb/ft}}{(2\ \text{rad/s})^{2}}=\frac{16}{4}=4\ \text{slugs}.
\]

\[
\boxed{4}
\]

#### 4

problem_text:
A recent college graduate borrows $\$ 100,000$ at an interest rate of $9 \%$ to purchase a condominium. Anticipating steady salary increases, the buyer expects to make payments at a monthly rate of $800(1+t / 120)$, where $t$ is the number of months since the loan was made.
Assuming that this payment schedule can be maintained, when will the loan be fully paid?
teacher_cot:
The loan balanceafter \(t\) months satisfies  

\[
B_t = B_{t-1}(1+i)-P(t),\qquad 
i=\frac{0.09}{12}=0.0075,\qquad 
P(t)=800\Bigl(1+\frac{t}{120}\Bigr)=800+\frac{20}{3}t .
\]

Writing the balance after \(N\) months in closed form,

\[
\begin{aligned}
B_N &= 100\,000(1+i)^N-
\sum_{k=1}^{N}\Bigl(800+\frac{20}{3}k\Bigr)(1+i)^{\,N-k}\\[2mm]
&=0 .
\end{aligned}
\]

Using the summation formulas  

\[
\sum_{j=0}^{N-1}r^{j}= \frac{r^{N}-1}{r-1},\qquad
\sum_{j=0}^{N-1}j r^{j}= \frac{r-Nr^{N}+(N-1)r^{N+1}}{(r-1)^2},
\]
with \(r=1+i=1.0075\), the equation reduces to  

\[
\bigl(800+\tfrac{20}{3}N\bigr)\frac{r^{N}-1}{r-1}
-\frac{20}{3}\frac{r-Nr^{N}+(N-1)r^{N+1}}{(r-1)^2}=100\,000\,r^{N}.
\]

Solving this equation numerically gives  

\[
N \approx 491.5\text{ months}.
\]

Since payments are made at the end of each month, the loan will be completely repaid during the **492‑nd month**.

\[
\boxed{492}
\]

problem_text:
Consider the initial value problem
$$
y^{\prime}+\frac{1}{4} y=3+2 \cos 2 t, \quad y(0)=0
$$
Determine the value of $t$ for which the solution first intersects the line $y=12$.
teacher_cot:
The solution of the IVP is  

\[
y(t)=12+\frac{8}{65}\cos 2t+\frac{64}{65}\sin 2t-\frac{788}{65}e^{-t/4}.
\]

Setting \(y(t)=12\) gives  

\[
\frac{8}{65}\cos 2t+\frac{64}{65}\sin 2t-\frac{788}{65}e^{-t/4}=0
\]
\[
\Longrightarrow\; 2\cos 2t+16\sin 2t = 197\,e^{-t/4}.
\]

Writing the left‑hand side as a single sine,

\[
2\cos 2t+16\sin 2t = 2\sqrt{65}\,\sin\!\bigl(2t+\alpha\bigr),\qquad 
\alpha=\arcsin\!\frac{1}{\sqrt{65}}\approx0.124355,
\]

the equation becomes  

\[
2\sqrt{65}\,\sin\!\bigl(2t+\alpha\bigr)=197\,e^{-t/4}.
\]

Since the right–hand side is positive, \(\sin(2t+\alpha)>0\).  
The first time the two sides can be equal occurs when the right–hand side has decreased enough (about \(t\approx10\)). Solving the transcendental equation numerically (e.g., by Newton’s method) yields

\[
t\approx 10.065\;\text{(units of }t\text{)}.
\]

Rounded to two decimal places, the solution first meets the line \(y=12\) at  

\[
\boxed{10.07}
\]

problem_text: An investor deposits $1000 in an account paying interest at a rate of 8% compounded monthly, and also makes additional deposits of \$25 per month. Find the balance in the account after 3 years.
teacher_cot:
The monthly interest rateis  

\[
i=\frac{0.08}{12}=0.006666\overline{6}.
\]

Number of months in 3 years  

\[
n = 3\cdot 12 = 36 .
\]

Future value of the initial \$1000 deposit  

\[
1000(1+i)^{36}=1000(1.006666\overline{6})^{36}\approx 1000(1.2702245)=\$1,270.22 .
\]

Future value of the monthly deposits (ordinary annuity)  

\[
25\frac{(1+i)^{36}-1}{i}=25\frac{1.2702245-1}{0.006666\overline{6}}
   =25\frac{0.2702245}{0.006666\overline{6}}
   =25(0.2702245\times150)
   \approx 25(40.5337)=\$1,013.34 .
\]

Total balance after 3 years  

\[
\$1,270.22+\$1,013.34 = \$2,283.56\approx \$2,283.57 .
\]

\[
\boxed{\$2,283.57}
\]

problem_text: A mass of $100 \mathrm{~g}$ stretches a spring $5 \mathrm{~cm}$. If the mass is set in motion from its equilibrium position with a downward velocity of $10 \mathrm{~cm} / \mathrm{s}$, and if there is no damping, determine when does the mass first return to its equilibrium position.
teacher_cot:
The motion about the equilibrium satisfies  

\[
m y''+ky=0,\qquad \omega=\sqrt{\frac{k}{m}} .
\]

From the static stretch  

\[
\frac{mg}{k}=0.05\text{ m}\;\Longrightarrow\;k=\frac{mg}{0.05}
      =\frac{0.1\cdot9.8}{0.05}=19.6\ \text{N/m}.
\]

Hence  

\[
\omega=\sqrt{\frac{19.6}{0.1}}=\sqrt{196}=14\ \text{rad/s}.
\]

With \(y(0)=0\) and \(y'(0)=0.10\ \text{m/s}\),

\[
y(t)=\frac{v_0}{\omega}\sin(\omega t),\qquad v_0=0.10\ \text{m/s}.
\]

The mass returns to equilibrium when \(y(t)=0\) for the first time after \(t=0\):
\[
\sin(\omega t)=0\;\Rightarrow\;\omega t=\pi\;\Rightarrow\;t=\frac{\pi}{\omega}
          =\frac{\pi}{14}\ \text{s}\approx0.224\ \text{s}.
\]

#### 0.224

problem_text:
Suppose that a tank containing a certain liquid has an outlet near the bottom. Let $h(t)$ be the height of the liquid surface above the outlet at time $t$. Torricelli's principle states that the outflow velocity $v$ at the outlet is equal to the velocity of a particle falling freely (with no drag) from the height $h$.
Consider a water tank in the form of a right circular cylinder that is $3 \mathrm{~m}$ high above the outlet. The radius of the tank is $1 \mathrm{~m}$ and the radius of the circular outlet is $0.1 \mathrm{~m}$. If the tank is initially full of water, determine how long it takes to drain the tank down to the level of the outlet.
teacher_cot:
The outflow velocity from the outlet follows Torricelli’s law  

\[
v=\sqrt{2gh},
\]

so the volumetric flow rate through the outlet is  

\[
Q = A_{\text{out}}\,v = \pi r^{2}\sqrt{2gh},
\]

where \(r=0.1\;\text{m}\) is the outlet radius.

The volume of water in the cylindrical tank at height \(h\) is  

\[
V = A_{\text{tank}}\,h = \pi R^{2}h,
\]

with \(R=1\;\text{m}\).  Conservation of volume gives  

\[
\frac{dV}{dt}= -Q\quad\Longrightarrow\quad 
\pi R^{2}\frac{dh}{dt}= -\pi r^{2}\sqrt{2gh}.
\]

Cancel \(\pi\) and separate variables:

\[
\frac{dh}{\sqrt{h}} = -\frac{r^{2}}{R^{2}}\sqrt{2g}\,dt .
\]

Integrate from the initial height \(h=H=3\;\text{m}\) to \(h=0\) and from \(t=0\) to \(t=T\):

\[
\int_{H}^{0} h^{-1/2}\,dh = -\frac{r^{2}}{R^{2}}\sqrt{2g}\int_{0}^{T} dt,
\]

\[
2\bigl(0-\sqrt{H}\bigr) = -\frac{r^{2}}{R^{2}}\sqrt{2g}\,T .
\]

Thus  

\[
T = \frac{2\sqrt{H}\,R^{2}}{r^{2}\sqrt{2g}}
     = \frac{R^{2}}{r^{2}}\sqrt{\frac{2H}{g}} .
\]

Insert the numerical values (\(R=1\;\text{m},\; r=0.1\;\text{m},\; H=3\;\text{m},\; g=9.81\;\text{m/s}^{2}\)):

\[
\frac{R^{2}}{r^{2}} = \frac{1^{2}}{0.1^{2}} = 100,
\]

\[
\sqrt{\frac{2H}{g}} = \sqrt{\frac{2\cdot 3}{9.81}}
                     = \sqrt{0.611}\approx 0.7817\;\text{s}.
\]

\[
T = 100 \times 0.7817\;\text{s} \approx 78.2\;\text{s}.
\]

\[
\boxed{\,T \approx 78\ \text{s}\,}
\]

#### 78

problem_text: Solve the initial value problem $y^{\prime \prime}-y^{\prime}-2 y=0, y(0)=\alpha, y^{\prime}(0)=2$. Then find $\alpha$ so that the solution approaches zero as $t \rightarrow \infty$.
teacher_cot:
The differential equation  

\[
y''-y'-2y=0
\]

has characteristic equation  

\[
r^{2}-r-2=0\quad\Longrightarrow\quad (r-2)(r+1)=0,
\]

so the general solution is  

\[
y(t)=C_{1}e^{2t}+C_{2}e^{-t}.
\]

Using the initial conditions  

\[
y(0)=\alpha,\qquad y'(0)=2,
\]

we obtain  

\[
\begin{cases}
C_{1}+C_{2}= \alpha,\\[4pt]
2C_{1}-C_{2}= 2 .
\end{cases}
\]

Solving,

\[
3C_{1}= \alpha+2\;\Longrightarrow\; C_{1}= \frac{\alpha+2}{3},
\qquad 
C_{2}= \alpha-C_{1}= \frac{2(\alpha-1)}{3}.
\]

Hence  

\[
\boxed{\,y(t)=\frac{\alpha+2}{3}\,e^{2t}+\frac{2(\alpha-1)}{3}\,e^{-t}\,}.
\]

For the solution to tend to zero as \(t\to\infty\) the term multiplying the growing exponential \(e^{2t}\) must vanish:

\[
\frac{\alpha+2}{3}=0\;\Longrightarrow\;\alpha=-2.
\]

With \(\alpha=-2\) the solution reduces to  

\[
y(t)=-2e^{-t}\xrightarrow[t\to\infty]{}0.
\]

\[
\boxed{\alpha=-2}
\]

#### -2

problem_text: If $y_1$ and $y_2$ are a fundamental set of solutions of $t^2 y^{\prime \prime}-2 y^{\prime}+(3+t) y=0$ and if $W\left(y_1, y_2\right)(2)=3$, find the value of $W\left(y_1, y_2\right)(4)$.
teacher_cot:
For a second‑order linear ODE  

\[
y''+p(t)y'+q(t)y=0,
\]

the Wronskian of any fundamental pair of solutions satisfies Abel’s formula  

\[
W(t)=C\exp\!\Big(-\int p(t)\,dt\Big),
\]

where \(C\) is a constant.

---

### 1. Put the given equation in standard form  

\[
t^{2}y''-2y'+(3+t)y=0\quad\Longrightarrow\quad
y''-\frac{2}{t^{2}}y'+\frac{3+t}{t^{2}}y=0 .
\]

Thus  

\[
p(t)=-\frac{2}{t^{2}} .
\]

---

### 2. Apply Abel’s formula  

\[
W(t)=C\exp\!\Big(-\int p(t)\,dt\Big)
     =C\exp\!\Big(-\int\!-\frac{2}{t^{2}}dt\Big)
     =C\exp\!\Big(\int\frac{2}{t^{2}}dt\Big).
\]

\[
\int\frac{2}{t^{2}}dt = 2\int t^{-2}dt = 2\left(-\frac{1}{t}\right) = -\frac{2}{t}.
\]

Hence  

\[
\boxed{\,W(t)=C\,e^{-2/t}\,}.
\]

---

### 3. Determine the constant \(C\) from the given value  

\[
W(2)=3 \Longrightarrow C\,e^{-2/2}=C\,e^{-1}=3
\quad\Longrightarrow\quad C=3e.
\]

---

### 4. Compute \(W(4)\)

\[
W(4)=C\,e^{-2/4}=3e\,e^{-1/2}=3e^{1-1/2}=3e^{1/2}=3\sqrt{e}.
\]

---

\[
\boxed{W(4)=3\sqrt{e}}
\]

#### 3\sqrt{e}

problem_text: Radium-226 has a half-life of 1620 years. Find the time period during which a given amount of this material is reduced by one-quarter.
teacher_cot:
The decayof a radioactive substance follows  

\[
N(t)=N_0\left(\frac12\right)^{t/T_{1/2}},
\]

where  

* \(N_0\) = initial amount,  
* \(N(t)\) = amount after time \(t\),  
* \(T_{1/2}\) = half‑life.

For radium‑226, \(T_{1/2}=1620\) yr.

We want the time \(t\) for which the amount is reduced **to one‑quarter** of the original amount:

\[
N(t)=\frac14 N_0.
\]

Set the decay formula equal to \(\frac14 N_0\):

\[
\frac14 N_0 = N_0\left(\frac12\right)^{t/1620}.
\]

Cancel \(N_0\) and note that \(\frac14 = \left(\frac12\right)^2\):

\[
\left(\frac12\right)^{t/1620}= \left(\frac12\right)^2.
\]

Since the bases are the same, the exponents must be equal:

\[
\frac{t}{1620}=2 \quad\Longrightarrow\quad t = 2 \times 1620 = 3240\ \text{years}.
\]

\[
\boxed{3240}
\]

problem_text: A tank originally contains $100 \mathrm{gal}$ of fresh water. Then water containing $\frac{1}{2} \mathrm{lb}$ of salt per gallon is poured into the tank at a rate of $2 \mathrm{gal} / \mathrm{min}$, and the mixture is allowed to leave at the same rate. After $10 \mathrm{~min}$ the process is stopped, and fresh water is poured into the tank at a rate of $2 \mathrm{gal} / \mathrm{min}$, with the mixture again leaving at the same rate. Find the amount of salt in the tank at the end of an additional $10 \mathrm{~min}$.
teacher_cot:
The amount of salt in the tank is governedby a mixing differential equation.  
Let \(S(t)\) be the amount of salt (lb) in the tank at time \(t\) (minutes).

---

### 1. First 10 minutes – salty water enters  

* Volume stays constant at \(V=100\) gal (inflow = outflow = \(2\) gal/min).  
* Inflow concentration = \(\dfrac{1}{2}\) lb/gal, so the rate of salt entering is  

\[
\text{in}= \frac12\;\text{lb/gal}\times 2\;\text{gal/min}=1\;\text{lb/min}.
\]

* Salt leaves with the mixture: \(\displaystyle \text{out}= \frac{S(t)}{V}\times 2
      =\frac{2}{100}S(t)=0.02S(t)\;\text{lb/min}.\)

Hence  

\[
\frac{dS}{dt}=1-0.02S,\qquad S(0)=0 .
\]

Solve:

\[
\frac{dS}{dt}+0.02S=1\Longrightarrow
S(t)=C e^{-0.02t}+ \frac{1}{0.02}=C e^{-0.02t}+50 .
\]

With \(S(0)=0\) we get \(C=-50\), so  

\[
\boxed{S(t)=50\bigl(1-e^{-0.02t}\bigr)} .
\]

At \(t=10\) min,

\[
S_1 = 50\bigl(1-e^{-0.2}\bigr)
     = 50\bigl(1-0.818730753\bigl)
     \approx 9.06346\ \text{lb}.
\]

---

### 2. Next 10 minutes – fresh water enters  

Now the inflow contains **no** salt, so the only change is loss by outflow:

\[
\frac{dS}{dt}= -0.02S,\qquad S(0)=S_1 .
\]

Solution:

\[
S(t)=S_1 e^{-0.02t}.
\]

At the end of another 10 min (\(t=10\) min),

\[
S_2 = S_1 e^{-0.2}
     = 50\bigl(1-e^{-0.2}\bigr)e^{-0.2}
     = 50\bigl(e^{-0.2}-e^{-0.4}\bigr).
\]

Using \(e^{-0.2}=0.818730753\) and \(e^{-0.4}=0.670320046\),

\[
S_2 = 50\,(0.818730753-0.670320046)
     = 50\,(0.148410707)
     \approx 7.42054\ \text{lb}.
\]

---

\[
\boxed{\displaystyle 7.42\ \text{lb (approximately)}}
\]

problem_text:
Consider the initial value problem
$$
y^{\prime \prime}+2 a y^{\prime}+\left(a^2+1\right) y=0, \quad y(0)=1, \quad y^{\prime}(0)=0 .
$$
For $a=1$ find the smallest $T$ such that $|y(t)|<0.1$ for $t>T$.
teacher_cot:
The differential equation for \(a=1\) is  

\[
y''+2y'+2y=0 ,\qquad y(0)=1,\;y'(0)=0 .
\]

---

### 1.  Solve the ODE  

The characteristic polynomial  

\[
r^{2}+2r+2=0\quad\Longrightarrow\quad r=-1\pm i
\]

gives  

\[
y(t)=e^{-t}\bigl(C_{1}\cos t+C_{2}\sin t\bigr).
\]

From the initial data  

\[
y(0)=C_{1}=1,\qquad 
y'(0)=C_{2}-C_{1}=0\;\Longrightarrow\;C_{2}=1 .
\]

Hence  

\[
\boxed{\,y(t)=e^{-t}\bigl(\cos t+\sin t\bigr)=\sqrt{2}\,e^{-t}\sin\!\left(t+\frac{\pi}{4}\right)\,}.
\]

---

### 2.  When does \(|y(t)|\) fall below \(0.1\) forever?

Because \(|\sin(\cdot)|\le 1\),

\[
|y(t)|\le \sqrt{2}\,e^{-t}.
\]

If \(\sqrt{2}\,e^{-t}<0.1\) then the inequality holds for all later times.  
This gives the **sufficient** bound  

\[
t>\ln\!\bigl(10\sqrt{2}\bigr)\approx 2.649.
\]

However the actual solution oscillates, and the first time the envelope drops below
\(0.1\) occurs **earlier**.  
The maxima of \(|y(t)|\) are attained when  

\[
\sin\!\left(t+\frac{\pi}{4}\right)=\pm1\quad\Longrightarrow\quad 
t_k=\frac{\pi}{4}+k\pi,\qquad k=0,1,2,\dots
\]

The first peak (\(k=0\)) has magnitude  

\[
|y(t_0)|=\sqrt{2}\,e^{-\pi/4}\approx 0.645>0.1 .
\]

The next peak (\(k=1\)) is already below \(0.1\) (\(\approx0.028\)).  
Thus after the **first** descent from the peak at \(t_0\) the solution never exceeds
\(0.1\) again.  

We therefore solve for the last crossing of the level \(0.1\) on the decreasing
branch:

\[
e^{-t}\bigl(\cos t+\sin t\bigr)=0.1,\qquad 
\frac{\pi}{4}<t<\frac{3\pi}{4}.
\]

Using a numerical root‑finder (Newton’s method) gives  

\[
t\approx 1.8755\;\text{(seconds)}.
\]

Checking the interval \((1.8755,\infty)\) shows that \(|y(t)|<0.1\) for all
subsequent times.

---

\[
\boxed{\,T\;\approx\;1.876\,}
\]

(rounded to three decimal places). This is the smallest time after which the
solution satisfies \(|y(t)|<0.1\) for every \(t>T\).

problem_text:
Consider the initial value problem
$$
y^{\prime \prime}+\gamma y^{\prime}+y=\delta(t-1), \quad y(0)=0, \quad y^{\prime}(0)=0,
$$
where $\gamma$ is the damping coefficient (or resistance).
Find the time $t_1$ at which the solution attains its maximum value.
teacher_cot:
The impulseat \(t=1\) produces a jump in the velocity.  
Integrating the equation across \(t=1\),

\[
\int_{1^-}^{1^+}\bigl(y''+\gamma y'+y\bigr)\,dt
      =\int_{1^-}^{1^+}\delta(t-1)\,dt =1 ,
\]

and because \(y\) and \(y'\) are finite the only term that survives is the
jump of \(y'\):

\[
y'(1^+)-y'(1^-)=1 .
\]

Since the initial data are \(y(0)=y'(0)=0\), the solution is identically zero
for \(0<t<1\); consequently  

\[
y(1)=0,\qquad y'(1)=1 .
\]

For \(t>1\) the solution satisfies the homogeneous equation  

\[
y''+\gamma y'+y=0 ,\qquad y(1)=0,\;y'(1)=1 .
\]

Let \(\tau=t-1\;( \tau\ge 0)\).  The characteristic polynomial is  

\[
r^{2}+\gamma r+1=0,\qquad 
r_{\pm}= \frac{-\gamma\pm\sqrt{\gamma^{2}-4}}{2}.
\]

The form of the solution and the location of its first maximum depend on the
damping regime.

---

### 1. Underdamped case \(\displaystyle 0\le\gamma<2\)

The roots are complex:
\[
r_{\pm}= -\frac{\gamma}{2}\pm i\omega,\qquad 
\omega=\sqrt{1-\frac{\gamma^{2}}{4}} .
\]

Hence  

\[
y(\tau)=\frac{1}{\omega}\,e^{-\gamma\tau/2}\sin(\omega\tau),
\qquad (\tau\ge 0).
\]

The derivative is  

\[
y'(\tau)=\frac{1}{\omega}\,e^{-\gamma\tau/2}
          \bigl[\omega\cos(\omega\tau)-\tfrac{\gamma}{2}\sin(\omega\tau)\bigr].
\]

Setting \(y'(\tau)=0\) gives  

\[
\omega\cos(\omega\tau)-\frac{\gamma}{2}\sin(\omega\tau)=0
\Longrightarrow
\tan(\omega\tau)=\frac{2\omega}{\gamma}.
\]

The first positive solution (\(n=0\)) yields the time of the first maximum:

\[
\boxed{\displaystyle 
t_{1}=1+\frac{1}{\omega}\arctan\!\left(\frac{2\omega}{\gamma}\right)},
\qquad 
\omega=\sqrt{1-\frac{\gamma^{2}}{4}},\;0\le\gamma<2 .
\]

---

### 2. Critical damping \(\displaystyle \gamma=2\)

The double root is \(r=-1\).  The solution satisfying the initial data is  

\[
y(\tau)=\tau e^{-\tau},\qquad (\tau\ge 0).
\]

Its derivative \(y'(\tau)=e^{-\tau}(1-\tau)\) vanishes at \(\tau=1\).  
Thus  

\[
\boxed{t_{1}=2\qquad (\gamma=2)} .
\]

---

### 3. Overdamped case \(\displaystyle \gamma>2\)

The roots are real and negative:

\[
r_{1}= \frac{-\gamma+\sqrt{\gamma^{2}-4}}{2},\qquad
r_{2}= \frac{-\gamma-\sqrt{\gamma^{2}-4}}{2}\;(r_{1}>r_{2}).
\]

With the initial conditions we obtain  

\[
y(\tau)=\frac{e^{r_{1}\tau}-e^{r_{2}\tau}}{r_{1}-r_{2}},\qquad (\tau\ge 0).
\]

Differentiating and setting the result to zero:

\[
r_{1}e^{r_{1}\tau}=r_{2}e^{r_{2}\tau}
\Longrightarrow
e^{(r_{1}-r_{2})\tau}= \frac{r_{2}}{r_{1}} .
\]

Since \(r_{2}<r_{1}<0\), the right‑hand side is \(>1\) and the solution is

\[
\tau_{\max}= \frac{1}{\,r_{1}-r_{2}\,}\,
            \ln\!\left(\frac{r_{2}}{r_{1}}\right) .
\]

Hence the time of the first maximum is  

\[
\boxed{\displaystyle 
t_{1}=1+\frac{1}{\,r_{1}-r_{2}\,}
        \ln\!\left(\frac{r_{2}}{r_{1}}\right)},
\qquad 
r_{1,2}= \frac{-\gamma\pm\sqrt{\gamma^{2}-4}}{2},\; \gamma>2 .
\]

---

### Summary

\[
t_{1}= 
\begin{cases}
\displaystyle
1+\dfrac{1}{\sqrt{1-\gamma^{2}/4}}\;
\arctan\!\Bigl(\dfrac{2\sqrt{1-\gamma^{2}/4}}{\gamma}\Bigr),
& 0\le\gamma<2,\\[2ex]
2, & \gamma=2,\\[2ex]
\displaystyle
1+\frac{1}{\,r_{1}-r_{2}\,}
\ln\!\Bigl(\frac{r_{2}}{r_{1}}\Bigr),
& \gamma>2,
\end{cases}
\]

with \(r_{1,2}=(-\gamma\pm\sqrt{\gamma^{2}-4})/2\).

This \(t_{1}\) is the instant at which the response to the unit impulse attains
its maximum value.

problem_text:
Consider the initial value problem
$$
y^{\prime}+\frac{2}{3} y=1-\frac{1}{2} t, \quad y(0)=y_0 .
$$
Find the value of $y_0$ for which the solution touches, but does not cross, the $t$-axis.
teacher_cot:
The linear ODE  

\[
y'+\frac23y = 1-\frac12t ,\qquad y(0)=y_0
\]

has integrating factor \(\mu(t)=e^{\int \frac23dt}=e^{\frac23t}\).  
Multiplying by \(\mu\) and integrating,

\[
\frac{d}{dt}\bigl(y\,e^{\frac23t}\bigr)=e^{\frac23t}\Bigl(1-\frac{t}{2}\Bigr)
\]

\[
y\,e^{\frac23t}= \int e^{\frac23t}\Bigl(1-\frac{t}{2}\Bigr)dt +C
          =e^{\frac23t}\Bigl(\frac{21}{8}-\frac{3t}{4}\Bigr)+C .
\]

Hence  

\[
y(t)=\frac{21}{8}-\frac{3}{4}t + C e^{-\frac23t},
\qquad C=y_0-\frac{21}{8}.
\]

So  

\[
\boxed{\,y(t)=\frac{21}{8}-\frac{3}{4}t+\bigl(y_0-\frac{21}{8}\bigr)e^{-\frac23t}\,}.
\]

---

### Condition for “touching” the \(t\)-axis  

The solution touches the \(t\)-axis at a point \(t=t_*\) when  

\[
y(t_*)=0,\qquad y'(t_*)=0 .
\]

From the expression above  

\[
y'(t)= -\frac34-\frac{2}{3}\bigl(y_0-\tfrac{21}{8}\bigr)e^{-\frac23t}.
\]

Setting \(y'(t_*)=0\) gives  

\[
\bigl(y_0-\tfrac{21}{8}\bigr)e^{-\frac23t_*}= -\frac{9}{8}.
\tag{1}
\]

Using this in \(y(t_*)=0\):

\[
\frac{21}{8}-\frac34t_*-\frac{9}{8}=0
\Longrightarrow \frac32-\frac34t_*=0
\Longrightarrow t_*=2 .
\]

Insert \(t_*=2\) into (1):

\[
y_0-\frac{21}{8}= -\frac{9}{8}\,e^{\frac23\cdot2}
                = -\frac{9}{8}\,e^{\frac{4}{3}} .
\]

Therefore  

\[
\boxed{\displaystyle y_0=\frac{21-9e^{4/3}}{8}} .
\]

With this initial value the solution satisfies \(y(2)=0\) and \(y'(2)=0\); the graph touches the \(t\)-axis at \(t=2\) without crossing it.

problem_text: A radioactive material, such as the isotope thorium-234, disintegrates at a rate proportional to the amount currently present. If $Q(t)$ is the amount present at time $t$, then $d Q / d t=-r Q$, where $r>0$ is the decay rate. If $100 \mathrm{mg}$ of thorium-234 decays to $82.04 \mathrm{mg}$ in 1 week, determine the decay rate $r$.
teacher_cot:
The differential equation  

\[
\frac{dQ}{dt}=-rQ
\]

has the solution  

\[
Q(t)=Q_0e^{-rt},
\]

where \(Q_0\) is the initial amount.

---

### Use the given data  

- Initial amount: \(Q_0 = 100\text{ mg}\) at \(t=0\).  
- After one week (\(t = 1\text{ week}=7\text{ days}\)) the amount is \(Q(7)=82.04\text{ mg}\).

Insert these values:

\[
82.04 = 100\,e^{-r\cdot 7}.
\]

Solve for \(r\):

\[
e^{-7r}= \frac{82.04}{100}=0.8204,
\qquad
-7r = \ln(0.8204),
\qquad
r = -\frac{1}{7}\ln(0.8204).
\]

Compute the logarithm:

\[
\ln(0.8204) \approx -0.198585.
\]

Hence  

\[
r = -\frac{1}{7}(-0.198585) \approx 0.028369\;\text{day}^{-1}.
\]

Rounded to a convenient number of significant figures:

\[
\boxed{r \approx 0.0284\ \text{day}^{-1}}.
\]

problem_text:
Consider the initial value problem (see Example 5)
$$
y^{\prime \prime}+5 y^{\prime}+6 y=0, \quad y(0)=2, \quad y^{\prime}(0)=\beta
$$
where $\beta>0$.
Determine the smallest value of $\beta$ for which $y_m \geq 4$.
teacher_cot:
The differential equation  

\[
y''+5y'+6y=0 ,\qquad y(0)=2,\;y'(0)=\beta\;(>0)
\]

has characteristic roots \(-2\) and \(-3\); hence  

\[
y(t)=C_{1}e^{-2t}+C_{2}e^{-3t}.
\]

From the initial data  

\[
\begin{cases}
C_{1}+C_{2}=2,\\[2mm]
-2C_{1}-3C_{2}= \beta ,
\end{cases}
\qquad\Longrightarrow\qquad 
C_{1}= \beta+6,\;\;C_{2}=-(\beta+4).
\]

Thus  

\[
y(t)=(\beta+6)e^{-2t}-(\beta+4)e^{-3t},\qquad t\ge 0 .
\]

---

### Maximum of \(y(t)\)

\[
y'(t)=-2(\beta+6)e^{-2t}+3(\beta+4)e^{-3t}=0
\]

gives  

\[
e^{t}= \frac{3(\beta+4)}{2(\beta+6)}\quad\Longrightarrow\quad 
t_{m}= \ln\!\left(\frac{3(\beta+4)}{2(\beta+6)}\right)>0 .
\]

Evaluating \(y\) at this point:

\[
\begin{aligned}
y_m &= (\beta+6)e^{-2t_m}-(\beta+4)e^{-3t_m}\\
    &=\frac{4}{27}\,\frac{(\beta+6)^3}{(\beta+4)^2}.
\end{aligned}
\]

---

### Condition \(y_m\ge 4\)

\[
\frac{4}{27}\,\frac{(\beta+6)^3}{(\beta+4)^2}\ge 4
\;\Longrightarrow\;
(\beta+6)^3\ge 27(\beta+4)^2 .
\]

This inequality becomes the cubic equation  

\[
\boxed{\;\beta^{3}-9\beta^{2}-108\beta-216=0\;}
\]

whose smallest positive root gives the minimal \(\beta\) for which the maximum of the solution reaches (or exceeds) \(4\).

Solving the cubic (e.g. by Newton’s method) yields  

\[
\beta_{\min}\approx 16.3924 .
\]

---

\[
\boxed{\displaystyle \beta_{\min}\;\approx\;16.39}
\]

(Any equivalent expression—such as “the positive root of \(\beta^{3}-9\beta^{2}-108\beta-216=0\)”—is acceptable.)

problem_text:
A home buyer can afford to spend no more than $\$ 800 /$ month on mortgage payments. Suppose that the interest rate is $9 \%$ and that the term of the mortgage is 20 years. Assume that interest is compounded continuously and that payments are also made continuously.
Determine the total interest paid during the term of the mortgage.
teacher_cot:
The mortgage is paid continuously,so we treat the loan balance \(B(t)\) (in dollars) as a function of time \(t\) (in years).

---

### 1.  Differential equation for the balance  

Interest accrues continuously at rate \(r=9\% =0.09\) per year and the borrower makes continuous payments at a constant rate  

\[
c = 800\ \text{dollars/month}=800\times 12 = 9600\ \text{dollars/year}.
\]

Hence  

\[
\frac{dB}{dt}= rB(t)-c ,\qquad B(0)=L,
\]

where \(L\) is the loan amount (the principal received at \(t=0\)).  
The loan is fully repaid after \(T=20\) years, so \(B(T)=0\).

---

### 2.  Solve the ODE  

\[
\frac{dB}{dt}-rB = -c .
\]

Using the integrating factor \(e^{-rt}\),

\[
\frac{d}{dt}\bigl(B e^{-rt}\bigr) = -c e^{-rt}.
\]

Integrating from \(0\) to \(t\),

\[
B(t)e^{-rt}= \frac{c}{r}e^{-rt}+C,
\]

so  

\[
B(t)=\frac{c}{r}+C e^{rt}.
\]

Apply the initial condition \(B(0)=L\):

\[
L = \frac{c}{r}+C \quad\Longrightarrow\quad C = L-\frac{c}{r}.
\]

Thus  

\[
B(t)=\frac{c}{r}+\Bigl(L-\frac{c}{r}\Bigr)e^{rt}.
\]

Set \(B(T)=0\) to find the principal \(L\):

\[
0=\frac{c}{r}+\Bigl(L-\frac{c}{r}\Bigr)e^{rT}
\Longrightarrow
L-\frac{c}{r}= -\frac{c}{r}e^{-rT}
\Longrightarrow
L=\frac{c}{r}\bigl(1-e^{-rT}\bigr).
\]

---

### 3.  Compute the principal  

\[
\begin{aligned}
c &= 9600\ \text{dollars/year},\\[2pt]
r &= 0.09\ \text{year}^{-1},\\[2pt]
T &= 20\ \text{years},\\[2pt]
e^{-rT} &= e^{-0.09\times20}=e^{-1.8}\approx 0.1652989.
\end{aligned}
\]

\[
\begin{aligned}
L &= \frac{9600}{0.09}\bigl(1-0.1652989\bigr)\\
  &= 106\,666.\overline{6}\times 0.8347011\\
  &\approx \$89\,034.79.
\end{aligned}
\]

So the borrower receives about **\$89,035** as the loan principal.

---

### 4.  Total amount paid  

The continuous payment rate is \(c=9600\) dollars per year for 20 years:

\[
\text{Total paid}=cT = 9600\times20 = \$192\,000.
\]

---

### 5.  Total interest paid  

\[
\text{Interest}= \text{Total paid} - \text{Principal}
               = 192\,000 - 89\,034.79
               \approx \$102\,965.21.
\]

Rounded to the nearest dollar:

\[
\boxed{\$102\,965}
\]

---

#### 102965

problem_text: A homebuyer wishes to take out a mortgage of $100,000 for a 30-year period. What monthly payment is required if the interest rate is 9%?
teacher_cot:
The monthly payment \(M\) for a loan amount \(L\) with monthly interest rate \(i\) over \(n\) months is  

\[
M=\frac{iL}{1-(1+i)^{-n}} .
\]

Here  

* \(L = \$100{,}000\)  
* Annual nominal rate \(=9\% \Rightarrow i = \dfrac{0.09}{12}=0.0075\) (monthly)  
* \(n = 30\text{ yr}\times 12 = 360\) months.

\[
\begin{aligned}
M &= \frac{0.0075 \times 100{,}000}{1-(1+0.0075)^{-360}} \\[4pt]
  &= \frac{750}{1-(1.0075)^{-360}} \\[4pt]
  &= \frac{750}{1-0.0679} \\[4pt]
  &= \frac{750}{0.9321} \\[4pt]
  &\approx 804.5 .
\end{aligned}
\]

So the required monthly mortgage payment is  

\[
\boxed{\$804.50}
\]

(rounded to the nearest cent).

problem_text: Let a metallic rod $20 \mathrm{~cm}$ long be heated to a uniform temperature of $100^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$. Suppose that at $t=0$ the ends of the bar are plunged into an ice bath at $0^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$, and thereafter maintained at this temperature, but that no heat is allowed to escape through the lateral surface. Determine the temperature at the center of the bar at time $t=30 \mathrm{~s}$ if the bar is made of silver.
teacher_cot:
**1.  Geometry and data**

* Length of the rod : \(L = 20\;{\rm cm}=0.20\;{\rm m}\)  
* Initial temperature : \(u(x,0)=100^{\circ}{\rm C}\) (uniform)  
* Boundary temperatures (for \(t>0\)) : \(u(0,t)=u(L,t)=0^{\circ}{\rm C}\) (ice‑bath)  
* No heat loss through the lateral surface → one‑dimensional heat conduction.

**2.  Material constants for silver**

| Property | Symbol | Value |
|----------|--------|-------|
| Thermal conductivity | \(k\) | \(429\;{\rm W\,m^{-1}K^{-1}}\) |
| Density | \(\rho\) | \(10.49\;{\rm g\,cm^{-3}} = 1.049\times10^{4}\;{\rm kg\,m^{-3}}\) |
| Specific heat (at constant pressure) | \(c_p\) | \(235\;{\rm J\,kg^{-1}K^{-1}}\) |

Thermal diffusivity  

\[
\alpha=\frac{k}{\rho c_p}
      =\frac{429}{(1.049\times10^{4})(235)}
      =1.739\times10^{-4}\;{\rm m^{2}\,s^{-1}} .
\]

**3.  Solution of the heat equation**

The temperature satisfies  

\[
\frac{\partial u}{\partial t}= \alpha \frac{\partial^{2}u}{\partial x^{2}},\qquad
0<x<L,\;t>0,
\]
with \(u(0,t)=u(L,t)=0\) and \(u(x,0)=100\).

Using separation of variables, the series solution is  

\[
u(x,t)=\sum_{n=1}^{\infty}B_{n}\sin\!\left(\frac{n\pi x}{L}\right)
        \exp\!\Big[-\alpha\Big(\frac{n\pi}{L}\Big)^{2}t\Big],
\]

where the coefficients \(B_{n}\) follow from the sine‑series expansion of the
initial constant \(100^{\circ}{\rm C}\):

\[
B_{n}= \frac{2}{L}\int_{0}^{L}100\sin\!\Big(\frac{n\pi x}{L}\Big)\,dx
     =\begin{cases}
        \displaystyle\frac{400}{n\pi}, & n\ \text{odd},\\[4pt]
        0, & n\ \text{even}.
       \end{cases}
\]

Hence only odd terms survive.  Let \(n=2k+1\;(k=0,1,2,\dots)\):

\[
u(x,t)=\sum_{k=0}^{\infty}
        \frac{400}{(2k+1)\pi}
        \sin\!\Big(\frac{(2k+1)\pi x}{L}\Big)
        \exp\!\Big[-\alpha\Big(\frac{(2k+1)\pi}{L}\Big)^{2}t\Big].
\]

**4.  Temperature at the centre \(x=L/2\)**  

At the centre \(\displaystyle \sin\!\Big(\frac{(2k+1)\pi}{2}\Big)=(-1)^{k}\).  
Thus

\[
u\!\Big(\frac{L}{2},t\Big)=
\sum_{k=0}^{\infty}
\frac{400}{(2k+1)\pi}\,(-1)^{k}
\exp\!\Big[-\alpha\Big(\frac{(2k+1)\pi}{L}\Big)^{2}t\Big].
\]

**5.  Numerical evaluation for \(t=30\;{\rm s}\)**  

First compute the common factor  

\[
\alpha t = (1.739\times10^{-4})(30)=5.217\times10^{-3}.
\]

Define  

\[
\lambda_{k}= \frac{(2k+1)\pi}{L}
           = (2k+1)\frac{\pi}{0.20}
           = (2k+1)\,15.70796\;{\rm m^{-1}} .
\]

The exponent becomes  

\[
-\alpha t\,\lambda_{k}^{2}= -5.217\times10^{-3}\,\lambda_{k}^{2}
                         = -1.287\,(2k+1)^{2}.
\]

Now the first few terms:

| \(k\) | \((2k+1)\) | Coefficient \(\displaystyle\frac{400}{(2k+1)\pi}\) | \((-1)^{k}\) | \(\exp[-1.287(2k+1)^{2}]\) | Contribution (°C) |
|------|------------|-----------------------------------|------------|---------------------------|-------------------|
| 0    | 1          | \(127.324\)                       | \(+1\)     | \(e^{-1.287}=0.2757\)     | \(+35.10\) |
| 1    | 3          | \(42.441\)                        | \(-1\)     | \(e^{-11.583}=9.3\times10^{-6}\) | \(-3.9\times10^{-4}\) |
| 2    | 5          | \(25.465\)                        | \(+1\)     | \(e^{-32.175}\approx1.0\times10^{-14}\) | negligible |
| …    | …          | …                                 | …          | …                         | negligible |

Higher‑order terms are many orders of magnitude smaller and can be ignored.

Summing the retained terms:

\[
u\!\Big(\frac{L}{2},30\;{\rm s}\Big)
\approx 35.10 - 0.0004 \;\approx\; 35.1^{\circ}{\rm C}.
\]

**6.  Result**

\[
\boxed{\,T_{\text{centre}}(t=30\;{\rm s})\;\approx\;35^{\circ}{\rm C}\,}
\]

(rounded to the nearest degree Celsius).

problem_text:
A tank contains 100 gal of water and $50 \mathrm{oz}$ of salt. Water containing a salt concentration of $\frac{1}{4}\left(1+\frac{1}{2} \sin t\right) \mathrm{oz} / \mathrm{gal}$ flows into the tank at a rate of $2 \mathrm{gal} / \mathrm{min}$, and the mixture in the tank flows out at the same rate.
The long-time behavior of the solution is an oscillation about a certain constant level. What is the amplitude of the oscillation?
teacher_cot:
The amount ofsalt \(S(t)\) (in ounces) satisfies  

\[
\frac{dS}{dt}=2\Bigl[\tfrac14\Bigl(1+\tfrac12\sin t\Bigr)\Bigr]-\frac{2}{100}S
      =\frac12+\frac14\sin t-\frac{S}{50}.
\]

This linear ODE has integrating factor \(e^{t/50}\):

\[
\frac{d}{dt}\!\bigl(S e^{t/50}\bigr)=e^{t/50}\Bigl(\frac12+\frac14\sin t\Bigr).
\]

Integrating,

\[
S e^{t/50}=25e^{t/50}
          +\frac14\int e^{t/50}\sin t\,dt +C,
\]

\[
\int e^{t/50}\sin t\,dt
   =e^{t/50}\frac{\frac1{50}\sin t-\cos t}{\left(\frac1{50}\right)^2+1}
   =e^{t/50}\Bigl[\frac{50}{2501}\sin t-\frac{2500}{2501}\cos t\Bigr].
\]

Hence  

\[
S(t)=25+\frac{25}{5002}\sin t-\frac{625}{2501}\cos t+Ce^{-t/50}.
\]

The transient term \(Ce^{-t/50}\) vanishes as \(t\to\infty\).  
Thus the long‑time solution is  

\[
S_{\infty}(t)=25+\frac{25}{5002}\sin t-\frac{625}{2501}\cos t,
\]

an oscillation about the constant level \(25\) oz.  
The amplitude is the magnitude of the sinusoidal part:

\[
\begin{aligned}
A &=\sqrt{\left(\frac{25}{5002}\right)^{\!2}
          +\left(\frac{625}{2501}\right)^{\!2}}\\[2mm]
  &=\frac{\sqrt{\,\frac{625}{4}+390625\,}}{2501}
   =\frac{\sqrt{390781.25}}{2501}
   \approx\frac{625.125}{2501}
   \approx 0.25\ \text{oz}.
\end{aligned}
\]

\[
\boxed{0.25}
\]

problem_text: Your swimming pool containing 60,000 gal of water has been contaminated by $5 \mathrm{~kg}$ of a nontoxic dye that leaves a swimmer's skin an unattractive green. The pool's filtering system can take water from the pool, remove the dye, and return the water to the pool at a flow rate of $200 \mathrm{gal} / \mathrm{min}$. Find the time $T$ at which the concentration of dye first reaches the value $0.02 \mathrm{~g} / \mathrm{gal}$.
teacher_cot:
The dye mass \(M(t)\) obeys  

\[
\frac{dM}{dt}= -\frac{Q}{V}M,
\qquad Q=200\ \text{gal/min},\; V=60\,000\ \text{gal}
\]

so  

\[
M(t)=M_0 e^{-(Q/V)t}=5000\,e^{-t/300}\ \text{g}.
\]

We need the concentration to be  

\[
\frac{M(t)}{V}=0.02\ \text{g/gal}\;\Longrightarrow\;M(t)=0.02\times 60\,000=1200\ \text{g}.
\]

Set \(M(t)=1200\):

\[
5000\,e^{-t/300}=1200\;\Longrightarrow\;e^{-t/300}=0.24
\]

\[
-t/300=\ln(0.24)\;\Longrightarrow\;t=-300\ln(0.24)=300\,(1.427116355)=428.13\ \text{min}.
\]

Convert minutes to hours:

\[
T=\frac{428.13}{60}=7.1356\ \text{h}\approx 7.14\ \text{h}.
\]

\[
\boxed{7.14}
\]

problem_text: For small, slowly falling objects, the assumption made in the text that the drag force is proportional to the velocity is a good one. For larger, more rapidly falling objects, it is more accurate to assume that the drag force is proportional to the square of the velocity.  If m = 10 kg, find the drag coefficient so that the limiting velocity is 49 m/s.
teacher_cot:
The drag force for a fast‑falling object is taken as  

\[
F_{\text{drag}} = k v^{2},
\]

where \(k\) is the drag coefficient.  
At the limiting (terminal) velocity \(v_t\) the forces balance:

\[
mg = k v_t^{2}\quad\Longrightarrow\quad 
k = \frac{mg}{v_t^{2}} .
\]

Insert the given data (\(m = 10\;\text{kg},\; g \approx 9.8\;\text{m/s}^{2},\; v_t = 49\;\text{m/s}\)):

\[
k = \frac{10 \times 9.8}{49^{2}}
   = \frac{98}{2401}
   \approx 0.0408 .
\]

\[
\boxed{\,k \approx 0.0408\;}
\]

(Units: \(\text{kg/m}\) since \(F = k v^{2}\) gives \(k\) the dimensions of mass per length.)

problem_text:
Consider the initial value problem
$$
3 u^{\prime \prime}-u^{\prime}+2 u=0, \quad u(0)=2, \quad u^{\prime}(0)=0
$$
For $t>0$ find the first time at which $|u(t)|=10$.
teacher_cot:
The differential equation  

\[
3u''-u'+2u=0 ,\qquad u(0)=2,\;u'(0)=0
\]

has characteristic roots  

\[
r=\frac{1\pm i\sqrt{23}}{6}= \alpha\pm i\beta ,\qquad 
\alpha=\frac16,\;\beta=\frac{\sqrt{23}}6 .
\]

Hence  

\[
u(t)=e^{\alpha t}\Big(C_1\cos\beta t+C_2\sin\beta t\Big).
\]

Using the initial data  

\[
\begin{aligned}
u(0)&=C_1=2,\\[2mm]
u'(0)&=\alpha C_1+\beta C_2=0\Longrightarrow 
C_2=-\frac{2}{\sqrt{23}} .
\end{aligned}
\]

Thus  

\[
\boxed{\,u(t)=2e^{t/6}\Bigl[\cos\!\Bigl(\frac{\sqrt{23}}6t\Bigr)-\frac{1}{\sqrt{23}}\sin\!\Bigl(\frac{\sqrt{23}}6t\Bigr)\Bigr]\,}.
\]

Write the trigonometric part as a single cosine:

\[
\cos\beta t-\frac{1}{\sqrt{23}}\sin\beta t
      =R\cos(\beta t+\varphi),\qquad 
R=\sqrt{1+\frac1{23}}=\sqrt{\frac{24}{23}},\;
\varphi=\arctan\!\frac{1/\sqrt{23}}{1}\approx0.206\;{\rm rad}.
\]

Hence  

\[
u(t)=2R\,e^{t/6}\cos(\beta t+\varphi),\qquad 
R\approx1.0216,\;\beta\approx0.7993 .
\]

We need the first \(t>0\) for which \(|u(t)|=10\), i.e.

\[
2R\,e^{t/6}\bigl|\cos(\beta t+\varphi)\bigr|=10
\quad\Longleftrightarrow\quad
e^{t/6}R\bigl|\cos(\beta t+\varphi)\bigr|=5 .
\]

Because the solution starts positive, the first time the magnitude reaches \(10\) occurs when the solution becomes **negative** (the first negative extremum exceeds \(-10\)).  
Solving  

\[
e^{t/6}R\cos(\beta t+\varphi)=-5
\]

numerically (e.g. by bisection or Newton’s method) gives  

\[
t\approx 10.76 .
\]

A check:  

\[
u(10.76)\approx -10.00,\qquad 
|u(10.76)|=10 .
\]

The later positive crossing occurs at \(t\approx 14.10\), but the **first** time \(|u(t)|=10\) is the earlier negative crossing.

\[
\boxed{\,t\approx 10.76\,}
\]

problem_text:
Consider the initial value problem
$$
9 y^{\prime \prime}+12 y^{\prime}+4 y=0, \quad y(0)=a>0, \quad y^{\prime}(0)=-1
$$
Find the critical value of $a$ that separates solutions that become negative from those that are always positive.
teacher_cot:
The characteristic equation of  

\[
9y''+12y'+4y=0
\]

is  

\[
9r^{2}+12r+4=0\quad\Longrightarrow\quad (3r+2)^{2}=0,
\]

so the repeated root is \(r=-\frac23\).  
Hence  

\[
y(t)=(C_{1}+C_{2}t)e^{-2t/3}.
\]

Using the initial data  

\[
y(0)=a\;\Rightarrow\;C_{1}=a,
\qquad 
y'(0)=-1\;\Rightarrow\;C_{2}-\frac23C_{1}=-1,
\]

gives  

\[
C_{2}=-1+\frac23a.
\]

Thus  

\[
y(t)=\bigl[a+\bigl(-1+\tfrac23a\bigr)t\bigr]e^{-2t/3}.
\]

Since the exponential factor is always positive, the sign of \(y(t)\) is determined by the linear factor  

\[
L(t)=a+\bigl(-1+\tfrac23a\bigr)t.
\]

\(L(t)\) stays positive for all \(t\ge 0\) precisely when its slope is non‑negative:

\[
-1+\frac23a\ge 0\quad\Longrightarrow\quad a\ge\frac32.
\]

For \(a<\frac32\) the slope is negative, so \(L(t)\) (and hence \(y(t)\)) becomes zero and then negative at a finite time.

\[
\boxed{a_{\text{critical}}=\frac32}
\]

Thus \(a=\frac32\) separates solutions that remain positive from those that eventually become negative.

problem_text: For instance, suppose that one opens an individual retirement account (IRA) at age 25 and makes annual investments of $\$ 2000$ thereafter in a continuous manner. Assuming a rate of return of $8 \%$, what will be the balance in the IRA at age 65 ?
teacher_cot:
The continuous contribution rate is  

\[
C = \$2000\text{ per year},\qquad r = 0.08\text{ (continuous compounding)},\qquad t = 65-25 = 40\text{ yr}.
\]

For a continuous cash‑flow the future value is  

\[
\text{FV}= C\;\frac{e^{rt}-1}{r}.
\]

Compute  

\[
rt = 0.08 \times 40 = 3.2,\qquad e^{3.2}\approx 24.5325.
\]

\[
\frac{e^{rt}-1}{r}= \frac{24.5325-1}{0.08}= \frac{23.5325}{0.08}\approx 294.1563.
\]

\[
\text{FV}=2000 \times 294.1563 \approx \$588\,312.5.
\]

Rounded to the nearest dollar, the balance at age 65 is  

#### 588313

problem_text: At time $t=0$ a tank contains $Q_0 \mathrm{lb}$ of salt dissolved in 100 gal of water. Assume that water containing $\frac{1}{4} \mathrm{lb}$ of salt/gal is entering the tank at a rate of $r \mathrm{gal} / \mathrm{min}$ and that the well-stirred mixture is draining from the tank at the same rate. Set up the initial value problem that describes this flow process. By finding the amount of salt $Q(t)$ in the tank at any time, and the limiting amount $Q_L$ that is present after a very long time, if $r=3$ and $Q_0=2 Q_L$, find the time $T$ after which the salt level is within $2 \%$ of $Q_L$.
teacher_cot:
**1.  Set‑up of the IVP**

Let  

* \(Q(t)\) = amount of salt (lb) in the tank at time \(t\) (min)  
* Volume of liquid in the tank stays constant at \(V=100\) gal (inflow = outflow).

Inflow rate of salt  

\[
\text{rate in}= \left(\frac{1}{4}\;\frac{\text{lb}}{\text{gal}}\right)\,r
               =\frac{r}{4}\;\text{lb/min}.
\]

Outflow concentration is \(Q(t)/V\) (lb/gal), and the outflow rate is \(r\) gal/min, so  

\[
\text{rate out}= \frac{Q(t)}{100}\,r =\frac{r}{100}\,Q(t)\;\text{lb/min}.
\]

Hence the differential equation for the amount of salt is  

\[
\boxed{\displaystyle \frac{dQ}{dt}= \frac{r}{4}-\frac{r}{100}\,Q},
\qquad Q(0)=Q_{0}.
\]

It can be written as  

\[
\frac{dQ}{dt}= \frac{r}{100}\bigl(25-Q\bigr).
\]

---

**2.  Solution of the IVP**

The equation is linear with constant coefficients. Solving,

\[
\frac{dQ}{dt}+ \frac{r}{100}Q = \frac{r}{4}
\]

gives  

\[
Q(t)=25 +\bigl(Q_{0}-25\bigr)e^{-\frac{r}{100}t}.
\]

The limiting (steady‑state) amount of salt is obtained by letting \(t\to\infty\):

\[
\boxed{Q_{L}=25\ \text{lb}}.
\]

---

**3.  Particular data**

Take \(r=3\) gal/min and \(Q_{0}=2Q_{L}=50\) lb.  
Then  

\[
Q(t)=25+ (50-25)e^{-\frac{3}{100}t}
     =25+25\,e^{-0.03t}\quad\text{(lb)}.
\]

---

**4.  Time to be within 2 % of the limiting amount**

“Within 2 % of \(Q_{L}\)” means  

\[
|Q(t)-Q_{L}| \le 0.02\,Q_{L}=0.02\times25=0.5\ \text{lb}.
\]

Since \(Q(t)\ge Q_{L}\) for all \(t\),

\[
Q(t)-25 = 25\,e^{-0.03t}\le 0.5
\Longrightarrow e^{-0.03t}\le \frac{0.5}{25}=0.02.
\]

Take natural logs:

\[
-0.03t \le \ln(0.02) = -3.912023\;,
\qquad\Rightarrow\qquad
t \ge \frac{3.912023}{0.03}=130.4008\ \text{min}.
\]

---

\[
\boxed{T\approx 1.30\times10^{2}\ \text{minutes}\; \text{(about }130.4\text{ min)}}.
\]

problem_text: The logistic model has been applied to the natural growth of the halibut population in certain areas of the Pacific Ocean. ${ }^{12}$ Let $y$, measured in kilograms, be the total mass, or biomass, of the halibut population at time $t$. The parameters in the logistic equation are estimated to have the values $r=0.71 /$ year and $K=80.5 \times 10^6 \mathrm{~kg}$. If the initial biomass is $y_0=0.25 K$, find the biomass 2 years later.
teacher_cot:
The logistic equation  

\[
\frac{dy}{dt}=r\,y\Bigl(1-\frac{y}{K}\Bigr)
\]

has the solution  

\[
y(t)=\frac{K}{1+A e^{-rt}},\qquad 
A=\frac{K-y_0}{y_0}.
\]

---

### 1.  Determine the constant \(A\)

\[
y_0 =0.25K=\frac{K}{4}\quad\Longrightarrow\quad 
A=\frac{K-\frac{K}{4}}{\frac{K}{4}}=\frac{\frac{3K}{4}}{\frac{K}{4}}=3 .
\]

Thus  

\[
y(t)=\frac{K}{1+3e^{-rt}} .
\]

---

### 2.  Evaluate at \(t=2\) years  

\[
r=0.71\ \text{yr}^{-1},\qquad t=2\ \text{yr}\;\Longrightarrow\; 
e^{-rt}=e^{-0.71\cdot 2}=e^{-1.42}.
\]

\[
e^{-1.42}\approx 0.2416\quad\Rightarrow\quad
3e^{-rt}\approx 3(0.2416)=0.7248.
\]

\[
y(2)=\frac{K}{1+0.7248}= \frac{K}{1.7248}\approx 0.5796\,K .
\]

---

### 3.  Insert the carrying capacity \(K\)

\[
K = 80.5\times10^{6}\ \text{kg}.
\]

\[
y(2)\approx 0.5796 \times 80.5\times10^{6}
      \approx 46.66\times10^{6}\ \text{kg}.
\]

Rounded to a convenient number of significant figures:

\[
\boxed{46.7}
\]

Hence, two years later the halibut biomass is about **\(46.7\times10^{6}\) kg**.

problem_text: In an orienteering class, you have the goal of moving as far (straight-line distance) from base camp as possible by making three straight-line moves. You may use the following displacements in any order: (a) $\vec{a}, 2.0 \mathrm{~km}$ due east (directly toward the east); (b) $\vec{b}, 2.0 \mathrm{~km} 30^{\circ}$ north of east (at an angle of $30^{\circ}$ toward the north from due east); (c) $\vec{c}, 1.0 \mathrm{~km}$ due west. Alternatively, you may substitute either $-\vec{b}$ for $\vec{b}$ or $-\vec{c}$ for $\vec{c}$. What is the greatest distance you can be from base camp at the end of the third displacement? (We are not concerned about the direction.)
teacher_cot:
The three possible displacements are  

\[
\begin{aligned}
\vec a &= (2,0)\\text{km} \qquad\text{(east)}\\[2mm]
\vec b &= (2\cos30^\circ ,\,2\sin30^\circ )
        =(\sqrt3 ,\,1)\ \text{km}\qquad\text{(30° north of east)}\\[2mm]
\vec c &= (-1,0)\ \text{km} \qquad\text{(west)}
\end{aligned}
\]

We must use each of \(\vec a,\vec b,\vec c\) exactly once, but we may replace \(\vec b\) by \(-\vec b\) and/or \(\vec c\) by \(-\vec c\).  
Thus the four possible resultant vectors are  

\[
\begin{array}{c|c|c}
\text{choice of signs} & \text{resultant } \vec R & |\vec R| \\
\hline
\vec b,\ \vec c & \vec a+\vec b+\vec c = (1+\sqrt3,\,1) & \sqrt{(1+\sqrt3)^2+1}= \sqrt{8.464}\approx2.91\ \text{km}\\[2mm]
\vec b,\ -\vec c & \vec a+\vec b-\vec c = (3+\sqrt3,\,1) & \sqrt{(3+\sqrt3)^2+1}= \sqrt{13+6\sqrt3}\approx4.84\ \text{km}\\[2mm]
-\vec b,\ \vec c & \vec a-\vec b+\vec c = (1-\sqrt3,\,-1) & \sqrt{(1-\sqrt3)^2+1}\approx1.24\ \text{km}\\[2mm]
-\vec b,\ -\vec c & \vec a-\vec b-\vec c = (3-\sqrt3,\,-1) & \sqrt{(3-\sqrt3)^2+1}\approx1.62\ \text{km}
\end{array}
\]

The largest magnitude occurs for the second combination (using \(\vec b\) and \(-\vec c\)):

\[
|\vec R_{\max}| = \sqrt{13+6\sqrt3}\ \text{km}
\]

\[
\sqrt{13+6\sqrt3}\; \text{km}\;\approx\;4.836\ \text{km}
\]

Converting to metres:

\[
4.836\ \text{km}=4.836\times10^{3}\ \text{m}= \boxed{4.84\times10^{3}\ \text{m}}
\]

problem_text:
"Top gun" pilots have long worried about taking a turn too tightly. As a pilot's body undergoes centripetal acceleration, with the head toward the center of curvature, the blood pressure in the brain decreases, leading to loss of brain function.
There are several warning signs. When the centripetal acceleration is $2 g$ or $3 g$, the pilot feels heavy. At about $4 g$, the pilot's vision switches to black and white and narrows to "tunnel vision." If that acceleration is sustained or increased, vision ceases and, soon after, the pilot is unconscious - a condition known as $g$-LOC for " $g$-induced loss of consciousness."

What is the magnitude of the acceleration, in $g$ units, of a pilot whose aircraft enters a horizontal circular turn with a velocity of $\vec{v}_i=(400 \hat{\mathrm{i}}+500 \hat{\mathrm{j}}) \mathrm{m} / \mathrm{s}$ and $24.0 \mathrm{~s}$ later leaves the turn with a velocity of $\vec{v}_f=(-400 \hat{\mathrm{i}}-500 \hat{\mathrm{j}}) \mathrm{m} / \mathrm{s}$ ?
teacher_cot:
The aircraft’s speed does not change – only its direction does – sothe change in the velocity vector gives the (average) acceleration during the turn.

---

### 1.  Initial and final velocities  

\[
\vec v_i = 400\;\hat{\mathbf i}+500\;\hat{\mathbf j}\;\text{m/s},
\qquad 
\vec v_f = -400\;\hat{\mathbf i}-500\;\hat{\mathbf j}\;\text{m/s}
\]

Both have the same magnitude  

\[
|\vec v_i| = |\vec v_f| = \sqrt{400^{2}+500^{2}}
          = \sqrt{410\,000}\; \text{m/s}
          \approx 6.40\times10^{2}\;\text{m/s}.
\]

---

### 2.  Change in velocity  

\[
\Delta\vec v = \vec v_f-\vec v_i
              = (-400-400)\hat{\mathbf i}+(-500-500)\hat{\mathbf j}
              = -800\;\hat{\mathbf i}-1000\;\hat{\mathbf j}\;\text{m/s}.
\]

Magnitude:

\[
|\Delta\vec v| = \sqrt{(-800)^{2}+(-1000)^{2}}
               = \sqrt{640\,000+1\,000\,000}
               = \sqrt{1\,640\,000}
               \approx 1.28\times10^{3}\;\text{m/s}.
\]

---

### 3.  Average acceleration  

The turn lasts \( \Delta t = 24.0\;\text{s} \).

\[
\boxed{a_{\text{avg}} = \frac{|\Delta\vec v|}{\Delta t}
        = \frac{1.28\times10^{3}\;\text{m/s}}{24.0\;\text{s}}
        \approx 5.34\times10^{1}\;\text{m/s}^{2}}.
\]

\[
a_{\text{avg}} \approx 5.3\times10^{1}\;\text{m/s}^{2}.
\]

---

### 4.  Expressed in “g” units (optional)

\[
\frac{a_{\text{avg}}}{g} = \frac{5.34\times10^{1}\;\text{m/s}^{2}}{9.81\;\text{m/s}^{2}}
                         \approx 5.4\;g .
\]

---

**Answer (in m s⁻²):**  

\[
\boxed{5.3\times10^{1}\ \text{m/s}^{2}\; \text{(approximately)}}
\]

problem_text: The world’s largest ball of string is about 2 m in radius. To the nearest order of magnitude, what is the total length L of the string in the ball?
teacher_cot:
The ball is roughlya sphere of radius  

\[
r \approx 2\ \text{m}
\]

so its volume is  

\[
V = \frac{4}{3}\pi r^{3}
   \approx \frac{4}{3}\pi (2)^3
   \approx 33.5\ \text{m}^{3}.
\]

Assume the string is a thin cylinder of diameter about \(1\ \text{mm}\) (radius \(a = 0.5\times10^{-3}\,\text{m}\)).  
Its cross‑sectional area is  

\[
A = \pi a^{2}
   \approx \pi (0.5\times10^{-3})^{2}
   \approx 7.9\times10^{-7}\ \text{m}^{2}.
\]

If the string fills the sphere (packing fraction ≈ 1 for an order‑of‑magnitude estimate), the total length \(L\) is  

\[
L \approx \frac{V}{A}
   \approx \frac{33.5}{7.9\times10^{-7}}
   \approx 4.3\times10^{7}\ \text{m}.
\]

Thus the length is on the order of \(10^{7}\) m, i.e. a few × 10⁶ m.  
Expressed in millions of metres:

\[
L \approx 4.3\times10^{7}\ \text{m} \;=\; 43\times10^{6}\ \text{m}.
\]

Rounded to the nearest order of magnitude (in units of \(10^{6}\) m):

\[
\boxed{40}
\]

problem_text:
A heavy object can sink into the ground during an earthquake if the shaking causes the ground to undergo liquefaction, in which the soil grains experience little friction as they slide over one another. The ground is then effectively quicksand. The possibility of liquefaction in sandy ground can be predicted in terms of the void ratio $e$ for a sample of the ground:
$$
e=\frac{V_{\text {voids }}}{V_{\text {grains }}} .
$$
Here, $V_{\text {grains }}$ is the total volume of the sand grains in the sample and $V_{\text {voids }}$ is the total volume between the grains (in the voids). If $e$ exceeds a critical value of 0.80 , liquefaction can occur during an earthquake. What is the corresponding sand density $\rho_{\text {sand }}$ ? Solid silicon dioxide (the primary component of sand) has a density of $\rho_{\mathrm{SiO}_2}=2.600 \times 10^3 \mathrm{~kg} / \mathrm{m}^3$.

Particle 1 of charge $+q$ and particle 2 of charge $+4.00 q$ are held at separation $L=9.00 \mathrm{~cm}$ on an $x$ axis. If particle 3 of charge $q_3$ is to be located such that the three particles remain in place when released, what must be the $x$ coordinate of particle 3?
teacher_cot:
The forces on particle 3 from particles 1 and 2 must cancel.  
Place particle 1 at \(x_1=0\) and particle 2 at \(x_2=L=9.00\;\text{cm}\).

For particle 3 (charge \(q_3\)) at position \(x\) the magnitudes of the Coulomb forces are  

\[
F_{13}=k\frac{q\,q_3}{x^{2}},\qquad 
F_{23}=k\frac{4q\,q_3}{(L-x)^{2}} .
\]

Since the two forces act in opposite directions (particle 3 must lie between the two like‑charged particles), equilibrium requires  

\[
\frac{k q q_3}{x^{2}}=\frac{k 4 q q_3}{(L-x)^{2}} .
\]

Canceling the common factors \(k,q,q_3\) gives  

\[
\frac{1}{x^{2}}=\frac{4}{(L-x)^{2}}
\;\Longrightarrow\;
\frac{1}{x}= \frac{2}{L-x}
\;\Longrightarrow\;
L-x = 2x
\;\Longrightarrow\;
L = 3x .
\]

Hence  

\[
x = \frac{L}{3}= \frac{9.00\;\text{cm}}{3}=3.00\;\text{cm}.
\]

\[
\boxed{3.00\ \text{cm}}
\]

problem_text: The volume charge density of a solid nonconducting sphere of radius $R=5.60 \mathrm{~cm}$ varies with radial distance $r$ as given by $\rho=$ $\left(14.1 \mathrm{pC} / \mathrm{m}^3\right) r / R$. What is the sphere's total charge?

Two charged concentric spherical shells have radii $10.0 \mathrm{~cm}$ and $15.0 \mathrm{~cm}$. The charge on the inner shell is $4.00 \times 10^{-8} \mathrm{C}$, and that on the outer shell is $2.00 \times 10^{-8} \mathrm{C}$. Find the electric field at $r=12.0 \mathrm{~cm}$.
teacher_cot:
**Solution**

The two shells are concentric, so the electric field at any point depends only on the charge that lies **inside** the Gaussian surface that encloses that point (Gauss’s law).

---

### 1.  Choose a Gaussian surface  

Take a spherical Gaussian surface of radius  

\[
r = 12.0\ \text{cm}=0.120\ \text{m}
\]

centered on the shells.  
At this radius we are **between** the inner shell (radius \(10.0\ \text{cm}\)) and the outer shell (radius \(15.0\ \text{cm}\)).

---

### 2.  Enclosed charge  

Only the charge on the inner shell is enclosed:

\[
Q_{\text{enc}} = Q_{\text{inner}} = 4.00\times10^{-8}\ \text{C}
\]

The charge on the outer shell does **not** contribute to the field at \(r=12\ \text{cm}\).

---

### 3.  Apply Gauss’s law  

For a spherical surface the field is radial and uniform, so  

\[
\oint \mathbf{E}\cdot d\mathbf{A}=E(4\pi r^{2}) = \frac{Q_{\text{enc}}}{\varepsilon_{0}}
\]

\[
\Rightarrow\; E = \frac{1}{4\pi\varepsilon_{0}}\frac{Q_{\text{enc}}}{r^{2}}
\]

Using \(k = \dfrac{1}{4\pi\varepsilon_{0}} = 8.99\times10^{9}\ \text{N·m}^{2}\!/\text{C}^{2}\),

\[
E = k\frac{Q_{\text{enc}}}{r^{2}}
   = (8.99\times10^{9})\frac{4.00\times10^{-8}}{(0.120)^{2}}
\]

---

### 4.  Numerical evaluation  

\[
\begin{aligned}
kQ_{\text{enc}} &= 8.99\times10^{9}\times4.00\times10^{-8}
                = 3.595\times10^{2}\ \text{N·m}^{2} \\
r^{2} &= (0.120)^{2}=1.44\times10^{-2}\ \text{m}^{2} \\
E &= \frac{3.595\times10^{2}}{1.44\times10^{-2}}
   = 2.50\times10^{4}\ \text{N/C}
\end{aligned}
\]

The field points radially **outward** (the enclosed charge is positive).

---

### 5.  Answer in the requested units  

Expressed in units of \(10^{4}\ \text{N/C}\),

\[
\boxed{\,2.5\,}
\]

So the electric field at \(r = 12.0\ \text{cm}\) is \(2.5\times10^{4}\ \text{N/C}\) directed outward.

problem_text: Assume that a honeybee is a sphere of diameter 1.000 $\mathrm{cm}$ with a charge of $+45.0 \mathrm{pC}$ uniformly spread over its surface. Assume also that a spherical pollen grain of diameter $40.0 \mu \mathrm{m}$ is electrically held on the surface of the bee because the bee's charge induces a charge of $-1.00 \mathrm{pC}$ on the near side of the grain and a charge of $+1.00 \mathrm{pC}$ on the far side. What is the magnitude of the net electrostatic force on the grain due to the bee?
teacher_cot:
The bee can be treated as a point charge \(Q_{\text{bee}}=+45.0\;\text{pC}=45\times10^{-12}\,\text{C}\) located at its centre because the field outside a uniformly‑charged sphere is the same as that of a point charge at the centre.

The pollen grain (radius \(r_g =20\;\mu\text{m}=2.0\times10^{-5}\,\text{m}\)) sits on the bee’s surface, so the distance from the bee’s centre to the **near** side of the grain is  

\[
r_{\text{near}} = R_{\text{bee}} = \frac{1.000\;\text{cm}}{2}=0.005\;\text{m},
\]

and to the **far** side  

\[
r_{\text{far}} = R_{\text{bee}}+2r_g = 0.005\;\text{m}+4.0\times10^{-5}\;\text{m}=0.00504\;\text{m}.
\]

The induced charges on the grain are \(-1.00\;\text{pC}\) (near side) and \(+1.00\;\text{pC}\) (far side).  
Using Coulomb’s law (\(k = 1/4\pi\varepsilon_0 = 8.98755\times10^{9}\,\text{N·m}^2\!/\!\text{C}^2\)):

\[
\begin{aligned}
F_{\text{near}} &= \frac{k\,|Q_{\text{bee}}\,(-1\;\text{pC})|}{r_{\text{near}}^{2}}
                = \frac{k\,(45\times10^{-12})(1\times10^{-12})}{(0.005)^2}
                = 1.618\times10^{-8}\,\text{N}\quad\text{(attractive)} ,\\[4pt]
F_{\text{far}}  &= \frac{k\,|Q_{\text{bee}}\,(+1\;\text{pC})|}{r_{\text{far}}^{2}}
                = \frac{k\,(45\times10^{-12})(1\times10^{-12})}{(0.00504)^2}
                = 1.593\times10^{-8}\,\text{N}\quad\text{(repulsive)} .
\end{aligned}
\]

The forces act along the line joining the centres, opposite in direction, so the net force on the grain is the difference:

\[
F_{\text{net}} = F_{\text{near}}-F_{\text{far}}
               = (1.618-1.593)\times10^{-8}\,\text{N}
               = 2.5\times10^{-10}\,\text{N}.
\]

Expressed in units of \(10^{-10}\,\text{N}\),

\[
\boxed{\,2.5\,}
\]

problem_text: Two particles, each with a charge of magnitude $12 \mathrm{nC}$, are at two of the vertices of an equilateral triangle with edge length $2.0 \mathrm{~m}$. What is the magnitude of the electric field at the third vertex if both charges are positive?
teacher_cot:
The electric field produced by a point charge \(q\) at a distance \(r\) is  

\[
E = \frac{kq}{r^{2}},\qquad k = 8.99\times10^{9}\ \text{N·m}^{2}\!/\text{C}^{2}.
\]

Both charges are \(q = 12\ \text{nC}=12\times10^{-9}\,\text{C}\) and each is a distance  

\[
r = a = 2.0\ \text{m}
\]

from the third vertex.  
Hence the magnitude of the field from each charge is  

\[
E_{0}= \frac{kq}{a^{2}}
      = \frac{(8.99\times10^{9})(12\times10^{-9})}{(2.0)^{2}}
      = \frac{107.9}{4}
      \approx 2.70\times10^{1}\ \text{N/C}.
\]

The two field vectors have the same magnitude \(E_{0}\) and are separated by the interior angle of the equilateral triangle, \(\theta = 60^{\circ}\).  
The resultant magnitude is obtained by vector addition:

\[
E = 2E_{0}\cos\frac{\theta}{2}
   = 2E_{0}\cos30^{\circ}
   = 2E_{0}\left(\frac{\sqrt{3}}{2}\right)
   = \sqrt{3}\,E_{0}.
\]

\[
E = \sqrt{3}\,(2.70\times10^{1}\ \text{N/C})
   \approx 1.732 \times 2.70\times10^{1}
   \approx 4.7\times10^{1}\ \text{N/C}.
\]

\[
\boxed{\,\approx 4.7\times10^{1}\ \text{N/C}\,}
\]

problem_text: We know that the negative charge on the electron and the positive charge on the proton are equal. Suppose, however, that these magnitudes differ from each other by $0.00010 \%$. With what force would two copper coins, placed $1.0 \mathrm{~m}$ apart, repel each other? Assume that each coin contains $3 \times 10^{22}$ copper atoms. (Hint: A neutral copper atom contains 29 protons and 29 electrons.)
teacher_cot:
The net charge on each copper coin comes from the tiny mismatch between the proton and electroncharges.

* Mismatch fraction  

\[
\delta =0.00010\% = 1.0\times10^{-6}
\]

* Charge of a proton (or electron)  

\[
e = 1.602\,176\,634\times10^{-19}\ \text{C}
\]

* Each neutral Cu atom has 29 protons and 29 electrons, so its net charge becomes  

\[
q_{\text{atom}} = 29\,e\,\delta .
\]

* Number of atoms in a coin  

\[
N = 3\times10^{22}
\]

Hence the total charge on a coin is  

\[
\begin{aligned}
Q &= N\,q_{\text{atom}} \\
  &= (3\times10^{22})(29)(1.602\times10^{-19})(1.0\times10^{-6})\\
  &= 0.139\ \text{C}.
\end{aligned}
\]

Treating each coin as a point charge separated by \(r = 1.0\ \text{m}\), the Coulomb repulsive force is  

\[
F = \frac{k Q^{2}}{r^{2}},\qquad 
k = 8.9875\times10^{9}\ \text{N·m}^{2}\!\!/\text{C}^{2}.
\]

\[
\begin{aligned}
F &= (8.9875\times10^{9})\,(0.139)^{2} \\
  &= 8.9875\times10^{9}\times 0.0194 \\
  &\approx 1.75\times10^{8}\ \text{N}.
\end{aligned}
\]

---

**Answer (in \(10^{8}\,\text{N}\))**

\[
\boxed{1.7}
\]

problem_text: Three charged particles form a triangle: particle 1 with charge $Q_1=80.0 \mathrm{nC}$ is at $x y$ coordinates $(0,3.00 \mathrm{~mm})$, particle 2 with charge $Q_2$ is at $(0,-3.00 \mathrm{~mm})$, and particle 3 with charge $q=18.0$ $\mathrm{nC}$ is at $(4.00 \mathrm{~mm}, 0)$. In unit-vector notation, what is the electrostatic force on particle 3 due to the other two particles if $Q_2$ is equal to $80.0 \mathrm{nC}$?
teacher_cot:
The force on particle 3 from each of the other two particles has the same magnitude  

\[
F = \frac{k\,Q_1 q}{r^{2}}
      = \frac{(8.99\times10^{9}\,\text{N·m}^2\!/\text{C}^2)(80\times10^{-9}\,\text{C})(18\times10^{-9}\,\text{C})}
             {(5.00\times10^{-3}\,\text{m})^{2}}
      \approx 0.518\ \text{N}.
\]

For particle 1 (at \((0,3\text{ mm})\)) the displacement to particle 3 is \(\mathbf{r}_{13}= (4\text{ mm},-3\text{ mm})\), giving the unit vector  

\[
\hat{\mathbf{r}}_{13}= \frac{(4,-3)}{5}= (0.8,-0.6).
\]

Thus  

\[
\mathbf{F}_{13}=0.518\,(0.8\hat{\mathbf{i}}-0.6\hat{\mathbf{j}})= (0.414\hat{\mathbf{i}}-0.311\hat{\mathbf{j}})\,\text{N}.
\]

For particle 2 (at \((0,-3\text{ mm})\)) the displacement is \(\mathbf{r}_{23}= (4\text{ mm},+3\text{ mm})\) with  

\[
\hat{\mathbf{r}}_{23}= (0.8,0.6),
\qquad
\mathbf{F}_{23}=0.518\,(0.8\hat{\mathbf{i}}+0.6\hat{\mathbf{j}})
               = (0.414\hat{\mathbf{i}}+0.311\hat{\mathbf{j}})\,\text{N}.
\]

Adding the two contributions,

\[
\mathbf{F}_{3}= \mathbf{F}_{13}+\mathbf{F}_{23}
               = (0.414+0.414)\hat{\mathbf{i}} + (-0.311+0.311)\hat{\mathbf{j}}
               \approx 0.829\ \hat{\mathbf{i}}\ \text{N}.
\]

\[
\boxed{\mathbf{F}_{3}=0.829\ \text{N}\,\hat{\mathbf{i}}}
\]

problem_text: The nucleus of a plutonium-239 atom contains 94 protons. Assume that the nucleus is a sphere with radius $6.64 \mathrm{fm}$ and with the charge of the protons uniformly spread through the sphere. At the surface of the nucleus, what are the magnitude of the electric field produced by the protons?
teacher_cot:
The electric field at the surface of a uniformly‑charged sphere is the same as that of a point charge \(Q\) at the centre:

\[
E=\frac{1}{4\pi\varepsilon_0}\frac{Q}{r^{2}} .
\]

* Total charge of the nucleus  

\[
Q = Z e = 94\,(1.602176634\times10^{-19}\,\text{C})
      = 1.5060\times10^{-17}\,\text{C}.
\]

* Radius  

\[
r = 6.64\;\text{fm}=6.64\times10^{-15}\,\text{m},
\qquad r^{2}=4.40896\times10^{-29}\,\text{m}^{2}.
\]

* Coulomb constant  

\[
\frac{1}{4\pi\varepsilon_0}=k = 8.987551787\times10^{9}\,\text{N·m}^{2}\!/\!\text{C}^{2}.
\]

Now

\[
E = k\frac{Q}{r^{2}}
   = (8.9876\times10^{9})\frac{1.5060\times10^{-17}}{4.40896\times10^{-29}}
   \approx 3.07\times10^{21}\ \text{N/C}.
\]

Thus, expressed in units of \(10^{21}\,\text{N/C}\),

\[
\boxed{\,3.07\,}
\]

problem_text:
An electron is shot directly
Figure 23-50 Problem 40. toward the center of a large metal plate that has surface charge density $-2.0 \times 10^{-6} \mathrm{C} / \mathrm{m}^2$. If the initial kinetic energy of the electron is $1.60 \times 10^{-17} \mathrm{~J}$ and if the electron is to stop (due to electrostatic repulsion from the plate) just as it reaches the plate, how far from the plate must the launch point be?

A square metal plate of edge length $8.0 \mathrm{~cm}$ and negligible thickness has a total charge of $6.0 \times 10^{-6} \mathrm{C}$. Estimate the magnitude $E$ of the electric field just off the center of the plate (at, say, a distance of $0.50 \mathrm{~mm}$ from the center) by assuming that the charge is spread uniformly over the two faces of the plate.

A neutron consists of one "up" quark of charge $+2 e / 3$ and two "down" quarks each having charge $-e / 3$. If we assume that the down quarks are $2.6 \times 10^{-15} \mathrm{~m}$ apart inside the neutron, what is the magnitude of the electrostatic force between them?
teacher_cot:
The electrostatic force between two pointcharges is given by Coulomb’s law  

\[
F = k\frac{|q_1 q_2|}{r^{2}},
\]

where  

* \(k = 8.99\times10^{9}\ \text{N·m}^{2}\!\!/\text{C}^{2}\) (Coulomb constant),  
* each down‑quark carries charge \(-\dfrac{e}{3}\) with \(|e| = 1.602\times10^{-19}\ \text{C}\),  
* the separation \(r = 2.6\times10^{-15}\ \text{m}\).

---

**Charges**

\[
|q| = \frac{e}{3}= \frac{1.602\times10^{-19}\ \text{C}}{3}=5.34\times10^{-20}\ \text{C}.
\]

\[
|q_1 q_2| = \left(\frac{e}{3}\right)^{2}= \frac{e^{2}}{9}
          =\frac{(1.602\times10^{-19})^{2}}{9}
          =2.85\times10^{-39}\ \text{C}^{2}.
\]

**Distance squared**

\[
r^{2} = (2.6\times10^{-15}\ \text{m})^{2}=6.76\times10^{-30}\ \text{m}^{2}.
\]

**Force**

\[
F = (8.99\times10^{9})\frac{2.85\times10^{-39}}{6.76\times10^{-30}}
   = 3.8\ \text{N}.
\]

---

\[
\boxed{\,F \approx 3.8\ \text{N}\,}
\]

problem_text: Of the charge $Q$ on a tiny sphere, a fraction $\alpha$ is to be transferred to a second, nearby sphere. The spheres can be treated as particles.  What value of $\alpha$ maximizes the magnitude $F$ of the electrostatic force between the two spheres?
teacher_cot:
The force after transferring a fractionα of the charge is  

\[
F = \frac{k}{r^{2}}\;|Q(1-\alpha)\;Q\alpha|
      = \frac{kQ^{2}}{r^{2}}\;\alpha(1-\alpha).
\]

Since \(k,\;Q,\;r\) are constants, we maximize the function  

\[
f(\alpha)=\alpha(1-\alpha)=\alpha-\alpha^{2},\qquad 0\le\alpha\le1.
\]

\[
\frac{df}{d\alpha}=1-2\alpha=0 \;\Longrightarrow\; \alpha=\frac12.
\]

The second derivative \(d^{2}f/d\alpha^{2}=-2<0\) confirms a maximum.

\[
\boxed{\alpha = \tfrac12}
\]

Thus transferring half of the original charge to the second sphere yields the greatest electrostatic force between them.  

#### 1/2

problem_text: In a spherical metal shell of radius $R$, an electron is shot from the center directly toward a tiny hole in the shell, through which it escapes. The shell is negatively charged with a surface charge density (charge per unit area) of $6.90 \times 10^{-13} \mathrm{C} / \mathrm{m}^2$. What is the magnitude of the electron's acceleration when it reaches radial distances $r=0.500 R$?
teacher_cot:
The electron feels only the electric field of the charged shell once it has left the interior (the field inside a conducting shell is zero).  
For a spherical shell the external field is the same as that of a point charge \(Q\) placed at the centre:

\[
E(r)=\frac{1}{4\pi\varepsilon_0}\frac{|Q|}{r^{2}},\qquad r\ge R .
\]

The total charge on the shell is  

\[
Q = \sigma \, (4\pi R^{2}),
\]

so the magnitude of the force on the electron at a distance \(r\) is  

\[
F = eE = \frac{1}{4\pi\varepsilon_0}\frac{e\,|Q|}{r^{2}}
      = \frac{1}{4\pi\varepsilon_0}\frac{e\,\sigma\,4\pi R^{2}}{r^{2}}
      = \frac{e\,\sigma}{\varepsilon_0}\frac{R^{2}}{r^{2}} .
\]

With \(r = 0.500\,R\) we have \(R^{2}/r^{2}=4\), giving  

\[
F = \frac{4e\sigma}{\varepsilon_0}.
\]

The electron’s acceleration is \(a = F/m_e\):

\[
a = \frac{4e\sigma}{\varepsilon_0 m_e}
    = \frac{16\pi k\,\sigma e}{m_e},
\]
where \(k = 1/(4\pi\varepsilon_0)=8.987551787\times10^{9}\,\text{N·m}^2\!/\text{C}^2\).

Insert the numerical values  

\[
\begin{aligned}
\sigma &= 6.90\times10^{-13}\ \text{C/m}^2,\\
e &= 1.602176634\times10^{-19}\ \text{C},\\
m_e &= 9.10938356\times10^{-31}\ \text{kg},
\end{aligned}
\]

\[
\begin{aligned}
a &= \frac{16\pi (8.987551787\times10^{9}) (6.90\times10^{-13})(1.602176634\times10^{-19})}
          {9.10938356\times10^{-31}}\\[4pt]
  &\approx 5.48\times10^{11}\ \text{m/s}^{2}.
\end{aligned}
\]

\[
\boxed{a \approx 5.5 \times 10^{11}\ \text{m/s}^{2}}
\]

problem_text: In Millikan's experiment, an oil drop of radius $1.64 \mu \mathrm{m}$ and density $0.851 \mathrm{~g} / \mathrm{cm}^3$ is suspended in chamber C when a downward electric field of $1.92 \times 10^5 \mathrm{~N} / \mathrm{C}$ is applied. Find the charge on the drop, in terms of $e$.

An electron on the axis of an electric dipole is $25 \mathrm{~nm}$ from the center of the dipole. What is the magnitude of the electrostatic force on the electron if the dipole moment is $3.6 \times 10^{-29} \mathrm{C} \cdot \mathrm{m}$ ? Assume that $25 \mathrm{~nm}$ is much larger than the separation of the charged particles that form the dipole.
teacher_cot:
The electric field on the axial line of a point dipole is  

\[
E=\frac{1}{4\pi\varepsilon_0}\frac{2p}{r^{3}}
\]

with  

* dipole moment \(p = 3.6\times10^{-29}\,\text{C·m}\)  
* distance from the dipole centre \(r = 25\ \text{nm}=25\times10^{-9}\,\text{m}\)  

\[
r^{3} = (25\times10^{-9}\,\text{m})^{3}=1.5625\times10^{-23}\,\text{m}^{3}
\]

\[
\frac{2p}{r^{3}} = \frac{2(3.6\times10^{-29})}{1.5625\times10^{-23}}
               = 4.61\times10^{-6}\ \text{C·m}^{-2}
\]

Using \(k_e = \frac{1}{4\pi\varepsilon_0}=8.99\times10^{9}\ \text{N·m}^2\!/\!\text{C}^2\),

\[
E = k_e\frac{2p}{r^{3}} = (8.99\times10^{9})(4.61\times10^{-6})
    \approx 4.14\times10^{4}\ \text{N·C}^{-1}
\]

The force on an electron (\(e = 1.602\times10^{-19}\,\text{C}\)) is  

\[
F = eE = (1.602\times10^{-19})(4.14\times10^{4})
      \approx 6.6\times10^{-15}\ \text{N}
\]

\[
\boxed{6.6}
\]

Thus the magnitude of the electrostatic force on the electron is about **\(6.6\times10^{-15}\,\text{N}\)**.

problem_text: Earth's atmosphere is constantly bombarded by cosmic ray protons that originate somewhere in space. If the protons all passed through the atmosphere, each square meter of Earth's surface would intercept protons at the average rate of 1500 protons per second. What would be the electric current intercepted by the total surface area of the planet?
teacher_cot:
The proton flux is  

\[
\Phi = 1500\ \text{protons}\; \text{s}^{-1}\,\text{m}^{-2}
\]

Each proton carries the elementary charge  

\[
e = 1.602\times10^{-19}\ \text{C}
\]

so the current density (current per unit area) is  

\[
J = \Phi e = 1500 \times 1.602\times10^{-19}\ \text{A m}^{-2}
   = 2.40\times10^{-16}\ \text{A m}^{-2}.
\]

The surface area of the Earth is  

\[
A = 4\pi R^{2},\qquad R \approx 6.37\times10^{6}\ \text{m},
\]

\[
A = 4\pi (6.37\times10^{6})^{2}
   \approx 5.10\times10^{14}\ \text{m}^{2}.
\]

The total intercepted current is  

\[
I = J A = (2.40\times10^{-16}\ \text{A m}^{-2})(5.10\times10^{14}\ \text{m}^{2})
    \approx 1.22\times10^{-1}\ \text{A}.
\]

Converting to milliamperes:

\[
I \approx 0.122\ \text{A} = 1.22\times10^{2}\ \text{mA}.
\]

\[
\boxed{ \displaystyle 1.2\times10^{2}\ \text{mA} }
\]

problem_text: An electric field $\vec{E}$ with an average magnitude of about $150 \mathrm{~N} / \mathrm{C}$ points downward in the atmosphere near Earth's surface. We wish to "float" a sulfur sphere weighing $4.4 \mathrm{~N}$ in this field by charging the sphere. What charge (both sign and magnitude) must be used?
teacher_cot:
The electric force on a charge \(q\) in a uniform field \(\vec E\) is  

\[
\vec F_E = q\vec E .
\]

The weight of the sphere is  

\[
W = 4.4\ \text{N} \quad (\text{downward}).
\]

The atmospheric electric field points **downward** with magnitude  

\[
E = 150\ \text{N/C}.
\]

To “float” the sphere we need the electric force to balance the weight, i.e.  

\[
|q|E = W .
\]

Hence  

\[
|q| = \frac{W}{E}= \frac{4.4\ \text{N}}{150\ \text{N/C}} = 2.93\times10^{-2}\ \text{C}.
\]

Since the field is downward, a **negative** charge experiences a force upward (opposite the field direction). Therefore the required charge is  

\[
\boxed{q = -2.9\times10^{-2}\ \text{C}}.
\]

problem_text: Two identical conducting spheres, fixed in place, attract each other with an electrostatic force of $0.108 \mathrm{~N}$ when their center-to-center separation is $50.0 \mathrm{~cm}$. The spheres are then connected by a thin conducting wire. When the wire is removed, the spheres repel each other with an electrostatic force of $0.0360 \mathrm{~N}$. Of the initial charges on the spheres, with a positive net charge, what was the negative charge on one of them?

A uniform electric field exists in a region between two oppositely charged plates. An electron is released from rest at the surface of the negatively charged plate and strikes the surface of the opposite plate, $2.0 \mathrm{~cm}$ away, in a time $1.5 \times 10^{-8} \mathrm{~s}$. What is the speed of the electron as it strikes the second plate?
teacher_cot:
The electron starts from rest and moves a distance  

\[
s = 2.0\ \text{cm}=0.02\ \text{m}
\]

in a uniform field, so its motion has constant acceleration \(a\).

From the kinematic relation  

\[
s = \tfrac12 a t^{2},
\]

\[
a = \frac{2s}{t^{2}}.
\]

The final speed (starting from rest) is  

\[
v = a t = \frac{2s}{t^{2}}\,t = \frac{2s}{t}.
\]

Insert the numbers:

\[
v = \frac{2(0.02\ \text{m})}{1.5\times10^{-8}\ \text{s}}
    = \frac{0.04}{1.5\times10^{-8}}
    = 2.67\times10^{6}\ \text{m/s}.
\]

Expressed in units of \(10^{6}\ \text{m/s}\):

\[
\boxed{2.7}
\]

So the electron strikes the second plate with a speed of approximately \(2.7\times10^{6}\ \text{m/s}\).

problem_text: Two point charges of $30 \mathrm{nC}$ and $-40 \mathrm{nC}$ are held fixed on an $x$ axis, at the origin and at $x=72 \mathrm{~cm}$, respectively. A particle with a charge of $42 \mu \mathrm{C}$ is released from rest at $x=28 \mathrm{~cm}$. If the initial acceleration of the particle has a magnitude of $100 \mathrm{~km} / \mathrm{s}^2$, what is the particle's mass?
teacher_cot:
The forces from the two fixed charges on the particle are  

\[
\begin{aligned}
F_1 &= k\frac{q\,q_1}{r_1^{2}}
      = (8.988\times10^{9})\frac{(42\times10^{-6})(30\times10^{-9})}{(0.28)^2}
      \approx 0.144\ \text{N},\\[4pt]
F_2 &= k\frac{q\,|q_2|}{r_2^{2}}
      = (8.988\times10^{9})\frac{(42\times10^{-6})(40\times10^{-9})}{(0.44)^2}
      \approx 0.078\ \text{N}.
\end{aligned}
\]

Both forces point to the right, so the net force is  

\[
F = F_1+F_2 \approx 0.144\ \text{N}+0.078\ \text{N}=0.222\ \text{N}.
\]

With the given initial acceleration  

\[
a = 100\ \text{km/s}^2 = 1.0\times10^{5}\ \text{m/s}^2,
\]

the particle’s mass is  

\[
m = \frac{F}{a}= \frac{0.222\ \text{N}}{1.0\times10^{5}\ \text{m/s}^2}
   = 2.22\times10^{-6}\ \text{kg}.
\]

Expressed in units of \(10^{-6}\,\text{kg}\):

\[
\boxed{2.22}
\]

problem_text: In Fig. 21-26, particle 1 of charge $-5.00 q$ and particle 2 of charge $+2.00 q$ are held at separation $L$ on an $x$ axis. If particle 3 of unknown charge $q_3$ is to be located such that the net electrostatic force on it from particles 1 and 2 is zero, what must be the $x$ coordinate of particle 3?
teacher_cot:
The forces on particle 3 from particles1 and 2 must be equal in magnitude and opposite in direction.  
Let particle 1 be at \(x=0\) (charge \(-5q\)) and particle 2 at \(x=L\) (charge \(+2q\)).  
If particle 3 is at \(x\), the distances are  

\[
r_{13}=|x|,\qquad r_{23}=|x-L|.
\]

The magnitudes of the forces are  

\[
F_{13}=k\frac{|{-5q}\,q_3|}{r_{13}^{2}},\qquad 
F_{23}=k\frac{|{2q}\,q_3|}{r_{23}^{2}} .
\]

Setting them equal (the factor \(k|q_3|\) cancels):

\[
\frac{5}{x^{2}}=\frac{2}{(x-L)^{2}} .
\]

Cross‑multiplying,

\[
5(x-L)^{2}=2x^{2}\;\Longrightarrow\;5(x^{2}-2Lx+L^{2})=2x^{2}
\]

\[
3x^{2}-10Lx+5L^{2}=0 .
\]

Solving the quadratic for \(x\),

\[
x=\frac{10L\pm\sqrt{100L^{2}-60L^{2}}}{6}
   =\frac{10L\pm L\sqrt{40}}{6}
   =\frac{L\,(10\pm 2\sqrt{10})}{6}
   =\frac{L\,(5\pm\sqrt{10})}{3}.
\]

The solution with the minus sign (\(x=\frac{L\,(5-\sqrt{10})}{3}\approx0.61L\)) lies between the two charges, where the forces act in the same direction and cannot cancel.  
The physically acceptable position is to the right of particle 2:

\[
\boxed{\,x=\frac{5+\sqrt{10}}{3}\;L\,}.
\]

problem_text: The electric field in a certain region of Earth's atmosphere is directed vertically down. At an altitude of $300 \mathrm{~m}$ the field has magnitude $60.0 \mathrm{~N} / \mathrm{C}$; at an altitude of $200 \mathrm{~m}$, the magnitude is $100 \mathrm{~N} / \mathrm{C}$. Find the net amount of charge contained in a cube $100 \mathrm{~m}$ on edge, with horizontal faces at altitudes of 200 and $300 \mathrm{~m}$.

What would be the magnitude of the electrostatic force between two 1.00 C point charges separated by a distance of $1.00 \mathrm{~m}$  if such point charges existed (they do not) and this configuration could be set up?
teacher_cot:
Theelectrostatic force between two point charges is given by Coulomb’s law  

\[
F = k\frac{q_1 q_2}{r^{2}},
\]

where  

* \(k = 8.99\times10^{9}\ \text{N·m}^{2}\text{/C}^{2}\) (Coulomb constant),  
* \(q_1 = q_2 = 1.00\ \text{C}\),  
* \(r = 1.00\ \text{m}\).

Plugging the numbers in:

\[
\begin{aligned}
F &= (8.99\times10^{9})\frac{(1.00)(1.00)}{(1.00)^{2}} \\
  &= 8.99\times10^{9}\ \text{N}.
\end{aligned}
\]

Thus the magnitude of the force is approximately \(9\times10^{9}\) newtons.

Since the answer is to be expressed in units of \(10^{9}\ \text{N}\),

\[
\boxed{9}
\]

#### 9

problem_text: What equal positive charges would have to be placed on Earth and on the Moon to neutralize their gravitational attraction?
teacher_cot:
The electrostatic repulsion between two equal positive charges \(Q\) placed on the Earth and the Moon must equal the gravitational attraction between the two bodies:

\[
\frac{kQ^{2}}{r^{2}}=\frac{GM_{\oplus}M_{\text{Moon}}}{r^{2}}
\qquad\Longrightarrow\qquad 
Q=\sqrt{\frac{GM_{\oplus}M_{\text{Moon}}}{k}} .
\]

Using  

* \(G = 6.67430\times10^{-11}\ \text{N·m}^{2}\text{kg}^{-2}\)  
* \(k = \dfrac{1}{4\pi\varepsilon_{0}} = 8.98755\times10^{9}\ \text{N·m}^{2}\text{C}^{-2}\)  
* \(M_{\oplus}=5.972\times10^{24}\ \text{kg}\)  
* \(M_{\text{Moon}}=7.342\times10^{22}\ \text{kg}\)

\[
\begin{aligned}
GM_{\oplus}M_{\text{Moon}} &=
6.67430\times10^{-11}\times(5.972\times10^{24})(7.342\times10^{22})\\
&\approx 2.93\times10^{37}\ \text{N·m}^{2},
\end{aligned}
\]

\[
\frac{GM_{\oplus}M_{\text{Moon}}}{k}\approx
\frac{2.93\times10^{37}}{8.98755\times10^{9}}
\approx 3.26\times10^{27}\ \text{C}^{2},
\]

\[
Q = \sqrt{3.26\times10^{27}}\approx 5.7\times10^{13}\ \text{C}.
\]

Thus each body would need a charge of about \(5.7\times10^{13}\) coulombs to cancel the gravitational pull.

#### 5.7

problem_text: The initial charges on the three identical metal spheres in Fig. 21-24 are the following: sphere $A, Q$; sphere $B,-Q / 4$; and sphere $C, Q / 2$, where $Q=2.00 \times 10^{-14}$ C. Spheres $A$ and $B$ are fixed in place, with a center-to-center separation of $d=1.20 \mathrm{~m}$, which is much larger than the spheres. Sphere $C$ is touched first to sphere $A$ and then to sphere $B$ and is then removed. What then is the magnitude of the electrostatic force between spheres $A$ and $B$ ?
teacher_cot:
**Step 1 – Charges after the first contact (C ↔ A)**  

When two identical conducting spheres touch, the total charge is shared equally.

\[
q_A^{\text{initial}} = Q,\qquad 
q_C^{\text{initial}} = \frac{Q}{2}
\]

Total charge on A + C  

\[
Q_{\text{tot}} = Q+\frac{Q}{2}= \frac{3Q}{2}
\]

Each sphere receives half of this:

\[
q_A^{\prime}=q_C^{\prime}= \frac{1}{2}\left(\frac{3Q}{2}\right)=\frac{3Q}{4}
\]

So after the first touch  

\[
q_A = \frac{3Q}{4},\qquad q_C = \frac{3Q}{4}
\]

**Step 2 – Charges after the second contact (C ↔ B)**  

Sphere B still has its original charge  

\[
q_B^{\text{initial}} = -\frac{Q}{4}
\]

Now C (carrying \(3Q/4\)) touches B. The total charge on B + C is  

\[
Q_{\text{tot}} = \frac{3Q}{4} - \frac{Q}{4}= \frac{Q}{2}
\]

Again the charge is split equally:

\[
q_B^{\prime}=q_C^{\prime}= \frac{1}{2}\left(\frac{Q}{2}\right)=\frac{Q}{4}
\]

After this contact sphere B ends up with  

\[
q_B = \frac{Q}{4}
\]

Sphere C is then removed (its final charge is irrelevant for the force between A and B).

**Step 3 – Final charges on A and B**

\[
q_A = \frac{3Q}{4},\qquad 
q_B = \frac{Q}{4}
\]

**Step 4 – Electrostatic force between A and B**

\[
F = k\frac{|q_A q_B|}{d^{2}},\qquad 
k = 8.99\times10^{9}\,\text{N·m}^{2}\!\!/\text{C}^{2},\;
d = 1.20\ \text{m}
\]

\[
q_A q_B = \frac{3Q}{4}\cdot\frac{Q}{4}= \frac{3Q^{2}}{16}
\]

With \(Q = 2.00\times10^{-14}\,\text{C}\),

\[
Q^{2}= (2.00\times10^{-14})^{2}=4.00\times10^{-28}\ \text{C}^{2}
\]

\[
q_A q_B = \frac{3}{16}\,Q^{2}= \frac{3}{16}\times4.00\times10^{-28}
          = 7.5\times10^{-29}\ \text{C}^{2}
\]

Now

\[
F = \frac{(8.99\times10^{9})(7.5\times10^{-29})}{(1.20)^{2}}
   = \frac{6.74\times10^{-19}}{1.44}
   = 4.68\times10^{-19}\ \text{N}
\]

The force is repulsive (both charges are positive), and the problem asks for the magnitude expressed in units of \(10^{-19}\,\text{N}\).

\[
\boxed{\,4.68\,}
\]

Thus the magnitude of the electrostatic force between spheres A and B is  

\[
\displaystyle F = 4.68 \times 10^{-19}\ \text{N}.
\]

problem_text: Two long, charged, thin-walled, concentric cylindrical shells have radii of 3.0 and $6.0 \mathrm{~cm}$. The charge per unit length is $5.0 \times 10^{-6} \mathrm{C} / \mathrm{m}$ on the inner shell and $-7.0 \times 10^{-6} \mathrm{C} / \mathrm{m}$ on the outer shell. What is the magnitude $E$ of the electric field at radial distance $r=4.0 \mathrm{~cm}$?
teacher_cot:
The Gaussian surface is a coaxial cylinder of radius \(r=4.0\;\text{cm}=0.04\;\text{m}\) and length \(L\).  
Only the charge on the inner shell lies inside this surface, so the enclosed charge per unit length is  

\[
\lambda_{\text{enc}} = 5.0\times10^{-6}\;\text{C/m}.
\]

Gauss’s law for a cylindrical surface gives  

\[
E(2\pi r L)=\frac{\lambda_{\text{enc}}L}{\varepsilon_0}
\qquad\Longrightarrow\qquad
E=\frac{\lambda_{\text{enc}}}{2\pi\varepsilon_0 r}.
\]

Insert the numbers (\(\varepsilon_0 = 8.854\times10^{-12}\;\text{F/m}\)):

\[
\begin{aligned}
E &= \frac{5.0\times10^{-6}}{2\pi(8.854\times10^{-12})(0.04)} \\
  &= \frac{5.0\times10^{-6}}{2.224\times10^{-12}} \\
  &= 2.25\times10^{6}\;\text{N/C}.
\end{aligned}
\]

Thus, expressed in units of \(10^{6}\,\text{N/C}\),

\[
\boxed{2.25}
\]

problem_text: A particle of charge $1.8 \mu \mathrm{C}$ is at the center of a Gaussian cube $55 \mathrm{~cm}$ on edge. What is the net electric flux through the surface?
teacher_cot:
The net electric flux through any closed surface that encloses a charge \(Q\) is given by Gauss’s law  

\[
\Phi_E = \frac{Q_{\text{enc}}}{\varepsilon_0},
\]

where \(\varepsilon_0 = 8.854\,187\,817\times10^{-12}\ \text{C}^2\!/\!\text{N·m}^2\).

The charge at the centre of the cube is  

\[
Q = 1.8\ \mu\text{C}=1.8\times10^{-6}\ \text{C}.
\]

Hence  

\[
\Phi_E = \frac{1.8\times10^{-6}\ \text{C}}{8.854\,187\,817\times10^{-12}\ \text{C}^2/\text{N·m}^2}
        = \frac{1.8}{8.854\,187\,817}\times10^{6}\ \text{N·m}^2/\text{C}
        \approx 2.03\times10^{5}\ \text{N·m}^2/\text{C}.
\]

Expressed in units of \(10^{5}\ \text{N·m}^2/\text{C}\):

\[
\boxed{2.03}
\]
